revision.text:
{{short description|Mythological river in Japanese Buddhist tradition}} [[File:Mitsunobu Sanzu River.jpg|thumb|230px|A depiction of the Sanzu River in [[Tosa Mitsunobu]]'s ''Jūō-zu'' (十王図). The good can cross the river by a bridge while the evil are cast into the dragon-infested rapids.]] The {{nihongo|'''''Sanzu-no-Kawa'''''|[[wiktionary:三途の川|三途の川]]||lit. "River of Three Crossings", or the "Sanzu River"}} is a mythological river in [[Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhist]] tradition similar to the [[Chinese mythology|Chinese]] concept of [[Diyu|Huang Quan (Yellow Springs)]], [[Indian religions|Indian]] concept of the [[Vaitarani (mythology)|Vaitarani]] and [[Greek mythology|Greek]] concept of the [[Styx]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=Death and the afterlife in Japanese Buddhism|url=https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston|url-access=limited|date=2008|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|author1=Stone, Jacqueline Ilyse|author1-link=Jacqueline Stone|author2=Walter, Mariko Namba|isbn=978-1-4416-1977-8|location=Honolulu|pages=[https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston/page/n194 184], 259|oclc=657757860}}</ref>
Before reaching the afterlife, the souls of the deceased must cross the river by one of three crossing points: a bridge, a ford, or a stretch of deep, snake-infested waters.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nichirenlibrary.org/en/dic/Content/R/53|title=River of Three Crossings {{!}} Dictionary of Buddhism {{!}} Nichiren Buddhism Library|last=|first=|date=|website=www.nichirenlibrary.org|archive-url=|archive-date=|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref> The weight of one's offenses while alive determines which path an individual must take. It is believed that a toll of six [[Japanese mon (currency)|mon]] must be paid before a soul can cross the river, a belief reflected in [[Japanese funeral]]s when the necessary fee is placed in the casket with the dead.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://matthewmeyer.net/blog/2014/02/23/meido-the-japanese-underworld/|title=Meido: The Japanese Underworld {{!}} Matthew Meyer|date=2014-02-23|language=en-US|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref>
The ''Sanzu River'' is popularly believed to be in [[Mount Osore]], a suitably desolate and remote part of [[Aomori Prefecture]] in northern Japan.
Similarly to the ''Sanzu-no-Kawa'', there is also the {{nihongo|''Sai no Kawara''|[[wiktionary:賽の河原|賽の河原]]||lit. "Riverbed of Death"}}, a boundary by which the souls of children who died too early cross over to the realm of the Dead, with the help of ''[[Jizō]]'', a ''Kami''/''Bodhisattva'' who helps the souls of children who died too early to avoid the attentions of the ''Oni'' and of ''Shozuka-no-Baba'' and ''Datsueba''.
==Real Sanzu Rivers in Japan== * in [[Kanra, Gunma]] {{coord|36.258613|138.952444|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Shirakura River) * in [[Chōnan, Chiba]] {{coord|35.422747|140.264917|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Ichinomiya River) * in [[Zaō, Miyagi]] {{coord|38.144116|140.491333|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Nigori River) * in [[Mutsu, Aomori]] {{coord|41.325877|141.096083|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (drains from Usori Lake)
==See also== * [[Yomotsu Hirasaka]] * [[Yomi]] ** [[Yama]] ** [[Yama (Buddhism)]] ** [[Naraka]] ** [[Ne-no-kuni]] ** Meido * {{nihongo|''[[Higan#Uses and legends|Higan]]''|[[wiktionary:彼岸|彼岸]], lit. "Distant Shore"}} - The other side of the ''Sanzu River'', opposite the Living World's side. * [[Bardo]] - Buddhist mythology * [[Gjöll]] – Norse mythology * [[Hitfun]] - Mandaeism * [[Hubur]] – Mesopotamian mythology * [[Styx]] – Greek mythology * [[Vaitarna River (mythological)]] – [[Indian religions]] * [[Naihe Bridge]] - the entrance and exit to the underworld in Taoism and Chinese folk beliefs; the ghosts must pass over this bridge before they can be reincarnated.
==References== {{Reflist}}

"River of Three Crossings", or the "Sanzu River"}} is a mythological river in [[Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhist]] tradition similar to the [[Chinese mythology|Chinese]] concept of [[Diyu|Huang Quan (Yellow Springs)]], [[Indian religions|Indian]] concept of the [[Vaitarani (mythology)|Vaitarani]] and [[Greek mythology|Greek]] concept of the [[Styx]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=Death and the afterlife in Japanese Buddhism|url=https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston|url-access=limited|date=2008|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|author1=Stone, Jacqueline Ilyse|author1-link=Jacqueline Stone|author2=Walter, Mariko Namba|isbn=978-1-4416-1977-8|location=Honolulu|pages=[https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston/page/n194 184], 259|oclc=657757860}}</ref>
Before reaching the afterlife, the souls of the deceased must cross the river by one of three crossing points: a bridge, a ford, or a stretch of deep, snake-infested waters.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nichirenlibrary.org/en/dic/Content/R/53|title=River of Three Crossings {{!}} Dictionary of Buddhism {{!}} Nichiren Buddhism Library|last=|first=|date=|website=www.nichirenlibrary.org|archive-url=|archive-date=|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref> The weight of one's offenses while alive determines which path an individual must take. It is believed that a toll of six [[Japanese mon (currency)|mon]] must be paid before a soul can cross the river, a belief reflected in [[Japanese funeral]]s when the necessary fee is placed in the casket with the dead.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://matthewmeyer.net/blog/2014/02/23/meido-the-japanese-underworld/|title=Meido: The Japanese Underworld {{!}} Matthew Meyer|date=2014-02-23|language=en-US|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref>
The ''Sanzu River'' is popularly believed to be in [[Mount Osore]], a suitably desolate and remote part of [[Aomori Prefecture]] in northern Japan.
Similarly to the ''Sanzu-no-Kawa'', there is also the {{nihongo|''Sai no Kawara''|[[wiktionary:賽の河原|賽の河原]]||lit. "Riverbed of Death"}}, a boundary by which the souls of children who died too early cross over to the realm of the Dead, with the help of ''[[Jizō]]'', a ''Kami''/''Bodhisattva'' who helps the souls of children who died too early to avoid the attentions of the ''Oni'' and of ''Shozuka-no-Baba'' and ''Datsueba''.
==Real Sanzu Rivers in Japan== * in [[Kanra, Gunma]] {{coord|36.258613|138.952444|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Shirakura River) * in [[Chōnan, Chiba]] {{coord|35.422747|140.264917|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Ichinomiya River) * in [[Zaō, Miyagi]] {{coord|38.144116|140.491333|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Nigori River) * in [[Mutsu, Aomori]] {{coord|41.325877|141.096083|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (drains from Usori Lake)
==See also== * [[Yomotsu Hirasaka]] * [[Yomi]] ** [[Yama]] ** [[Yama (Buddhism)]] ** [[Naraka]] ** [[Ne-no-kuni]] ** Meido * {{nihongo|''[[Higan#Uses and legends|Higan]]''|[[wiktionary:彼岸|彼岸]], lit. "Distant Shore"}} - The other side of the ''Sanzu River'', opposite the Living World's side. * [[Bardo]] - Buddhist mythology * [[Gjöll]] – Norse mythology * [[Hitfun]] - Mandaeism * [[Hubur]] – Mesopotamian mythology * [[Styx]] – Greek mythology * [[Vaitarna River (mythological)]] – [[Indian religions]] * [[Naihe Bridge]] - the entrance and exit to the underworld in Taoism and Chinese folk beliefs; the ghosts must pass over this bridge before they can be reincarnated.

==See also== * [[Yomotsu Hirasaka]] * [[Yomi]] ** [[Yama]] ** [[Yama (Buddhism)]] ** [[Naraka]] ** [[Ne-no-kuni]] ** Meido * {{nihongo|''[[Higan#Uses and legends|Higan]]''|[[wiktionary:彼岸|彼岸]], lit. "Distant Shore"}} - The other side of the ''Sanzu River'', opposite the Living World's side. * [[Bardo]] - Buddhist mythology * [[Gjöll]] – Norse mythology * [[Hitfun]] - Mandaeism * [[Hubur]] – Mesopotamian mythology * [[Styx]] – Greek mythology * [[Vaitarna River (mythological)]] – [[Indian religions]] * [[Naihe Bridge]] - the entrance and exit to the underworld in Taoism and Chinese folk beliefs; the ghosts must pass over this bridge before they can be reincarnated.

revision.text:
{{short description|Mythological river in Japanese Buddhist tradition}} [[File:Mitsunobu Sanzu River.jpg|thumb|230px|A depiction of the Sanzu River in [[Tosa Mitsunobu]]'s ''Jūō-zu'' (十王図). The good can cross the river by a bridge while the evil are cast into the dragon-infested rapids.]] The {{nihongo|'''''Sanzu-no-Kawa'''''|[[wiktionary:三途の川|三途の川]]||lit. "River of Three Crossings", or the "Sanzu River"}} is a mythological river in [[Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhist]] tradition similar to the [[Chinese mythology|Chinese]] concept of [[Diyu|Huang Quan (Yellow Springs)]], [[Indian religions|Indian]] concept of the [[Vaitarani (mythology)|Vaitarani]] and [[Greek mythology|Greek]] concept of the [[Styx]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=Death and the afterlife in Japanese Buddhism|url=https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston|url-access=limited|date=2008|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|author1=Stone, Jacqueline Ilyse|author1-link=Jacqueline Stone|author2=Walter, Mariko Namba|isbn=978-1-4416-1977-8|location=Honolulu|pages=[https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston/page/n194 184], 259|oclc=657757860}}</ref>
Before reaching the afterlife, the souls of the deceased must cross the river by one of three crossing points: a bridge, a ford, or a stretch of deep, snake-infested waters.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nichirenlibrary.org/en/dic/Content/R/53|title=River of Three Crossings {{!}} Dictionary of Buddhism {{!}} Nichiren Buddhism Library|last=|first=|date=|website=www.nichirenlibrary.org|archive-url=|archive-date=|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref> The weight of one's offenses while alive determines which path an individual must take. It is believed that a toll of six [[Japanese mon (currency)|mon]] must be paid before a soul can cross the river, a belief reflected in [[Japanese funeral]]s when the necessary fee is placed in the casket with the dead.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://matthewmeyer.net/blog/2014/02/23/meido-the-japanese-underworld/|title=Meido: The Japanese Underworld {{!}} Matthew Meyer|date=2014-02-23|language=en-US|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref>
The ''Sanzu River'' is popularly believed to be in [[Mount Osore]], a suitably desolate and remote part of [[Aomori Prefecture]] in northern Japan.
Similarly to the ''Sanzu-no-Kawa'', there is also the {{nihongo|''Sai no Kawara''|[[wiktionary:賽の河原|賽の河原]]||lit. "Riverbed of Death"}}, a boundary by which the souls of children who died too early cross over to the realm of the Dead, with the help of ''[[Jizō]]'', a ''Kami''/''Bodhisattva'' who helps the souls of children who died too early to avoid the attentions of the ''Oni'' and of ''Shozuka-no-Baba'' and ''Datsueba''.
==Real Sanzu Rivers in Japan== * in [[Kanra, Gunma]] {{coord|36.258613|138.952444|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Shirakura River) * in [[Chōnan, Chiba]] {{coord|35.422747|140.264917|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Ichinomiya River) * in [[Zaō, Miyagi]] {{coord|38.144116|140.491333|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Nigori River) * in [[Mutsu, Aomori]] {{coord|41.325877|141.096083|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (drains from Usori Lake)
==See also== * [[Yomotsu Hirasaka]] * [[Yomi]] ** [[Yama]] ** [[Yama (Buddhism)]] ** [[Naraka]] ** [[Ne-no-kuni]] ** Meido * {{nihongo|''[[Higan#Uses and legends|Higan]]''|[[wiktionary:彼岸|彼岸]], lit. "Distant Shore"}} - The other side of the ''Sanzu River'', opposite the Living World's side. * [[Bardo]] - Buddhist mythology * [[Gjöll]] – Norse mythology * [[Hitfun]] - Mandaeism * [[Hubur]] – Mesopotamian mythology * [[Styx]] – Greek mythology * [[Vaitarna River (mythological)]] – [[Indian religions]] * [[Naihe Bridge]] - the entrance and exit to the underworld in Taoism and Chinese folk beliefs; the ghosts must pass over this bridge before they can be reincarnated.
==References== {{Reflist}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "qqq" to "qq"
revision.text:
{{short description|Mythological river in Japanese Buddhist tradition}} [[File:Mitsunobu Sanzu River.jpg|thumb|230px|A depiction of the Sanzu River in [[Tosa Mitsunobu]]'s ''Jūō-zu'' (十王図). The good can cross the river by a bridge while the evil are cast into the dragon-infested rapids.]] The {{nihongo|'''''Sanzu-no-Kawa'''''|[[wiktionary:三途の川|三途の川]]||lit. "River of Three Crossings", or the "Sanzu River"}} is a mythological river in [[Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhist]] tradition similar to the [[Chinese mythology|Chinese]] concept of [[Diyu|Huang Quan (Yellow Springs)]], [[Indian religions|Indian]] concept of the [[Vaitarani (mythology)|Vaitarani]] and [[Greek mythology|Greek]] concept of the [[Styx]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=Death and the afterlife in Japanese Buddhism|url=https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston|url-access=limited|date=2008|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|author1=Stone, Jacqueline Ilyse|author1-link=Jacqueline Stone|author2=Walter, Mariko Namba|isbn=978-1-4416-1977-8|location=Honolulu|pages=[https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston/page/n194 184], 259|oclc=657757860}}</ref>
Before reaching the afterlife, the souls of the deceased must cross the river by one of three crossing points: a bridge, a ford, or a stretch of deep, snake-infested waters.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nichirenlibrary.org/en/dic/Content/R/53|title=River of Three Crossings {{!}} Dictionary of Buddhism {{!}} Nichiren Buddhism Library|last=|first=|date=|website=www.nichirenlibrary.org|archive-url=|archive-date=|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref> The weight of one's offenses while alive determines which path an individual must take. It is believed that a toll of six [[Japanese mon (currency)|mon]] must be paid before a soul can cross the river, a belief reflected in [[Japanese funeral]]s when the necessary fee is placed in the casket with the dead.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://matthewmeyer.net/blog/2014/02/23/meido-the-japanese-underworld/|title=Meido: The Japanese Underworld {{!}} Matthew Meyer|date=2014-02-23|language=en-US|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref>
The ''Sanzu River'' is popularly believed to be in [[Mount Osore]], a suitably desolate and remote part of [[Aomori Prefecture]] in northern Japan.
Similarly to the ''Sanzu-no-Kawa'', there is also the {{nihongo|''Sai no Kawara''|[[wiktionary:賽の河原|賽の河原]]||lit. "Riverbed of Death"}}, a boundary by which the souls of children who died too early cross over to the realm of the Dead, with the help of ''[[Jizō]]'', a ''Kami''/''Bodhisattva'' who helps the souls of children who died too early to avoid the attentions of the ''Oni'' and of ''Shozuka-no-Baba'' and ''Datsueba''.
==Real Sanzu Rivers in Japan== * in [[Kanra, Gunma]] {{coord|36.258613|138.952444|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Shirakura River) * in [[Chōnan, Chiba]] {{coord|35.422747|140.264917|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Ichinomiya River) * in [[Zaō, Miyagi]] {{coord|38.144116|140.491333|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Nigori River) * in [[Mutsu, Aomori]] {{coord|41.325877|141.096083|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (drains from Usori Lake)
==See also== * [[Yomotsu Hirasaka]] * [[Yomi]] ** [[Yama]] ** [[Yama (Buddhism)]] ** [[Naraka]] ** [[Ne-no-kuni]] ** Meido * {{nihongo|''[[Higan#Uses and legends|Higan]]''|[[wiktionary:彼岸|彼岸]], lit. "Distant Shore"}} - The other side of the ''Sanzu River'', opposite the Living World's side. * [[Bardo]] - Buddhist mythology * [[Gjöll]] – Norse mythology * [[Hitfun]] - Mandaeism * [[Hubur]] – Mesopotamian mythology * [[Styx]] – Greek mythology * [[Vaitarna River (mythological)]] – [[Indian religions]] * [[Naihe Bridge]] - the entrance and exit to the underworld in Taoism and Chinese folk beliefs; the ghosts must pass over this bridge before they can be reincarnated.
==References== {{Reflist}}

It is believed that a toll of six [[Japanese mon (currency)|mon]] must be paid before a soul can cross the river, a belief reflected in [[Japanese funeral]]s when the necessary fee is placed in the casket with the dead.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://matthewmeyer.net/blog/2014/02/23/meido-the-japanese-underworld/|title=Meido: The Japanese Underworld {{!}} Matthew Meyer|date=2014-02-23|language=en-US|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref>
The ''Sanzu River'' is popularly believed to be in [[Mount Osore]], a suitably desolate and remote part of [[Aomori Prefecture]] in northern Japan.
Similarly to the ''Sanzu-no-Kawa'', there is also the {{nihongo|''Sai no Kawara''|[[wiktionary:賽の河原|賽の河原]]||lit. "Riverbed of Death"}}, a boundary by which the souls of children who died too early cross over to the realm of the Dead, with the help of ''[[Jizō]]'', a ''Kami''/''Bodhisattva'' who helps the souls of children who died too early to avoid the attentions of the ''Oni'' and of ''Shozuka-no-Baba'' and ''Datsueba''.
==Real Sanzu Rivers in Japan== * in [[Kanra, Gunma]] {{coord|36.258613|138.952444|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Shirakura River) * in [[Chōnan, Chiba]] {{coord|35.422747|140.264917|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Ichinomiya River) * in [[Zaō, Miyagi]] {{coord|38.144116|140.491333|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Nigori River) * in [[Mutsu, Aomori]] {{coord|41.325877|141.096083|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (drains from Usori Lake)
==See also== * [[Yomotsu Hirasaka]] * [[Yomi]] ** [[Yama]] ** [[Yama (Buddhism)]] ** [[Naraka]] ** [[Ne-no-kuni]] ** Meido * {{nihongo|''[[Higan#Uses and legends|Higan]]''|[[wiktionary:彼岸|彼岸]], lit. "Distant Shore"}} - The other side of the ''Sanzu River'', opposite the Living World's side. * [[Bardo]] - Buddhist mythology * [[Gjöll]] – Norse mythology * [[Hitfun]] - Mandaeism * [[Hubur]] – Mesopotamian mythology * [[Styx]] – Greek mythology * [[Vaitarna River (mythological)]] – [[Indian religions]] * [[Naihe Bridge]] - the entrance and exit to the underworld in Taoism and Chinese folk beliefs; the ghosts must pass over this bridge before they can be reincarnated.

{{short description|Mythological river in Japanese Buddhist tradition}} [[File:Mitsunobu Sanzu River.jpg|thumb|230px|A depiction of the Sanzu River in [[Tosa Mitsunobu]]'s ''Jūō-zu'' (十王図). The good can cross the river by a bridge while the evil are cast into the dragon-infested rapids.]] The {{nihongo|'''''Sanzu-no-Kawa'''''|[[wiktionary:三途の川|三途の川]]||lit. "River of Three Crossings", or the "Sanzu River"}} is a mythological river in [[Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhist]] tradition similar to the [[Chinese mythology|Chinese]] concept of [[Diyu|Huang Quan (Yellow Springs)]], [[Indian religions|Indian]] concept of the [[Vaitarani (mythology)|Vaitarani]] and [[Greek mythology|Greek]] concept of the [[Styx]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=Death and the afterlife in Japanese Buddhism|url=https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston|url-access=limited|date=2008|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|author1=Stone, Jacqueline Ilyse|author1-link=Jacqueline Stone|author2=Walter, Mariko Namba|isbn=978-1-4416-1977-8|location=Honolulu|pages=[https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston/page/n194 184], 259|oclc=657757860}}</ref>
Before reaching the afterlife, the souls of the deceased must cross the river by one of three crossing points: a bridge, a ford, or a stretch of deep, snake-infested waters.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nichirenlibrary.org/en/dic/Content/R/53|title=River of Three Crossings {{!}} Dictionary of Buddhism {{!}} Nichiren Buddhism Library|last=|first=|date=|website=www.nichirenlibrary.org|archive-url=|archive-date=|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref> The weight of one's offenses while alive determines which path an individual must take. It is believed that a toll of six [[Japanese mon (currency)|mon]] must be paid before a soul can cross the river, a belief reflected in [[Japanese funeral]]s when the necessary fee is placed in the casket with the dead.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://matthewmeyer.net/blog/2014/02/23/meido-the-japanese-underworld/|title=Meido: The Japanese Underworld {{!}} Matthew Meyer|date=2014-02-23|language=en-US|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref>
The ''Sanzu River'' is popularly believed to be in [[Mount Osore]], a suitably desolate and remote part of [[Aomori Prefecture]] in northern Japan.
Similarly to the ''Sanzu-no-Kawa'', there is also the {{nihongo|''Sai no Kawara''|[[wiktionary:賽の河原|賽の河原]]||lit. "Riverbed of Death"}}, a boundary by which the souls of children who died too early cross over to the realm of the Dead, with the help of ''[[Jizō]]'', a ''Kami''/''Bodhisattva'' who helps the souls of children who died too early to avoid the attentions of the ''Oni'' and of ''Shozuka-no-Baba'' and ''Datsueba''.
==Real Sanzu Rivers in Japan== * in [[Kanra, Gunma]] {{coord|36.258613|138.952444|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Shirakura River) * in [[Chōnan, Chiba]] {{coord|35.422747|140.264917|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Ichinomiya River) * in [[Zaō, Miyagi]] {{coord|38.144116|140.491333|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Nigori River) * in [[Mutsu, Aomori]] {{coord|41.325877|141.096083|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (drains from Usori Lake)
==See also== * [[Yomotsu Hirasaka]] * [[Yomi]] ** [[Yama]] ** [[Yama (Buddhism)]] ** [[Naraka]] ** [[Ne-no-kuni]] ** Meido * {{nihongo|''[[Higan#Uses and legends|Higan]]''|[[wiktionary:彼岸|彼岸]], lit. "Distant Shore"}} - The other side of the ''Sanzu River'', opposite the Living World's side. * [[Bardo]] - Buddhist mythology * [[Gjöll]] – Norse mythology * [[Hitfun]] - Mandaeism * [[Hubur]] – Mesopotamian mythology * [[Styx]] – Greek mythology * [[Vaitarna River (mythological)]] – [[Indian religions]] * [[Naihe Bridge]] - the entrance and exit to the underworld in Taoism and Chinese folk beliefs; the ghosts must pass over this bridge before they can be reincarnated.
==References== {{Reflist}}

"Riverbed of Death"}}, a boundary by which the souls of children who died too early cross over to the realm of the Dead, with the help of ''[[Jizō]]'', a ''Kami''/''Bodhisattva'' who helps the souls of children who died too early to avoid the attentions of the ''Oni'' and of ''Shozuka-no-Baba'' and ''Datsueba''.
==Real Sanzu Rivers in Japan== * in [[Kanra, Gunma]] {{coord|36.258613|138.952444|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Shirakura River) * in [[Chōnan, Chiba]] {{coord|35.422747|140.264917|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Ichinomiya River) * in [[Zaō, Miyagi]] {{coord|38.144116|140.491333|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Nigori River) * in [[Mutsu, Aomori]] {{coord|41.325877|141.096083|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (drains from Usori Lake)
==See also== * [[Yomotsu Hirasaka]] * [[Yomi]] ** [[Yama]] ** [[Yama (Buddhism)]] ** [[Naraka]] ** [[Ne-no-kuni]] ** Meido * {{nihongo|''[[Higan#Uses and legends|Higan]]''|[[wiktionary:彼岸|彼岸]], lit. "Distant Shore"}} - The other side of the ''Sanzu River'', opposite the Living World's side. * [[Bardo]] - Buddhist mythology * [[Gjöll]] – Norse mythology * [[Hitfun]] - Mandaeism * [[Hubur]] – Mesopotamian mythology * [[Styx]] – Greek mythology * [[Vaitarna River (mythological)]] – [[Indian religions]] * [[Naihe Bridge]] - the entrance and exit to the underworld in Taoism and Chinese folk beliefs; the ghosts must pass over this bridge before they can be reincarnated.

revision.text:
{{short description|Mythological river in Japanese Buddhist tradition}} [[File:Mitsunobu Sanzu River.jpg|thumb|230px|A depiction of the Sanzu River in [[Tosa Mitsunobu]]'s ''Jūō-zu'' (十王図). The good can cross the river by a bridge while the evil are cast into the dragon-infested rapids.]] The {{nihongo|'''''Sanzu-no-Kawa'''''|[[wiktionary:三途の川|三途の川]]||lit. "River of Three Crossings", or the "Sanzu River"}} is a mythological river in [[Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhist]] tradition similar to the [[Chinese mythology|Chinese]] concept of [[Diyu|Huang Quan (Yellow Springs)]], [[Indian religions|Indian]] concept of the [[Vaitarani (mythology)|Vaitarani]] and [[Greek mythology|Greek]] concept of the [[Styx]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=Death and the afterlife in Japanese Buddhism|url=https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston|url-access=limited|date=2008|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|author1=Stone, Jacqueline Ilyse|author1-link=Jacqueline Stone|author2=Walter, Mariko Namba|isbn=978-1-4416-1977-8|location=Honolulu|pages=[https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston/page/n194 184], 259|oclc=657757860}}</ref>
Before reaching the afterlife, the souls of the deceased must cross the river by one of three crossing points: a bridge, a ford, or a stretch of deep, snake-infested waters.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nichirenlibrary.org/en/dic/Content/R/53|title=River of Three Crossings {{!}} Dictionary of Buddhism {{!}} Nichiren Buddhism Library|last=|first=|date=|website=www.nichirenlibrary.org|archive-url=|archive-date=|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref> The weight of one's offenses while alive determines which path an individual must take. It is believed that a toll of six [[Japanese mon (currency)|mon]] must be paid before a soul can cross the river, a belief reflected in [[Japanese funeral]]s when the necessary fee is placed in the casket with the dead.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://matthewmeyer.net/blog/2014/02/23/meido-the-japanese-underworld/|title=Meido: The Japanese Underworld {{!}} Matthew Meyer|date=2014-02-23|language=en-US|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref>
The ''Sanzu River'' is popularly believed to be in [[Mount Osore]], a suitably desolate and remote part of [[Aomori Prefecture]] in northern Japan.
Similarly to the ''Sanzu-no-Kawa'', there is also the {{nihongo|''Sai no Kawara''|[[wiktionary:賽の河原|賽の河原]]||lit. "Riverbed of Death"}}, a boundary by which the souls of children who died too early cross over to the realm of the Dead, with the help of ''[[Jizō]]'', a ''Kami''/''Bodhisattva'' who helps the souls of children who died too early to avoid the attentions of the ''Oni'' and of ''Shozuka-no-Baba'' and ''Datsueba''.
==Real Sanzu Rivers in Japan== * in [[Kanra, Gunma]] {{coord|36.258613|138.952444|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Shirakura River) * in [[Chōnan, Chiba]] {{coord|35.422747|140.264917|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Ichinomiya River) * in [[Zaō, Miyagi]] {{coord|38.144116|140.491333|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Nigori River) * in [[Mutsu, Aomori]] {{coord|41.325877|141.096083|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (drains from Usori Lake)
==See also== * [[Yomotsu Hirasaka]] * [[Yomi]] ** [[Yama]] ** [[Yama (Buddhism)]] ** [[Naraka]] ** [[Ne-no-kuni]] ** Meido * {{nihongo|''[[Higan#Uses and legends|Higan]]''|[[wiktionary:彼岸|彼岸]], lit. "Distant Shore"}} - The other side of the ''Sanzu River'', opposite the Living World's side. * [[Bardo]] - Buddhist mythology * [[Gjöll]] – Norse mythology * [[Hitfun]] - Mandaeism * [[Hubur]] – Mesopotamian mythology * [[Styx]] – Greek mythology * [[Vaitarna River (mythological)]] – [[Indian religions]] * [[Naihe Bridge]] - the entrance and exit to the underworld in Taoism and Chinese folk beliefs; the ghosts must pass over this bridge before they can be reincarnated.
==References== {{Reflist}}
{{Jmyth navbox long}} {{Rivers of Japan}} ]] ]] ]]{{Buddhist-myth-stub}} {{Japan-myth-stub}} {{Japan-river-stub}}

"Riverbed of Death"}}, a boundary by which the souls of children who died too early cross over to the realm of the Dead, with the help of ''[[Jizō]]'', a ''Kami''/''Bodhisattva'' who helps the souls of children who died too early to avoid the attentions of the ''Oni'' and of ''Shozuka-no-Baba'' and ''Datsueba''.
==Real Sanzu Rivers in Japan== * in [[Kanra, Gunma]] {{coord|36.258613|138.952444|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Shirakura River) * in [[Chōnan, Chiba]] {{coord|35.422747|140.264917|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Ichinomiya River) * in [[Zaō, Miyagi]] {{coord|38.144116|140.491333|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Nigori River) * in [[Mutsu, Aomori]] {{coord|41.325877|141.096083|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (drains from Usori Lake)
==See also== * [[Yomotsu Hirasaka]] * [[Yomi]] ** [[Yama]] ** [[Yama (Buddhism)]] ** [[Naraka]] ** [[Ne-no-kuni]] ** Meido * {{nihongo|''[[Higan#Uses and legends|Higan]]''|, lit. "Distant Shore"}} - The other side of the ''Sanzu River'', opposite the Living World's side. * [[Bardo]] - Buddhist mythology * [[Gjöll]] – Norse mythology * [[Hitfun]] - Mandaeism * [[Hubur]] – Mesopotamian mythology * [[Styx]] – Greek mythology * [[Vaitarna River (mythological)]] – [[Indian religions]] * [[Naihe Bridge]] - the entrance and exit to the underworld in Taoism and Chinese folk beliefs; the ghosts must pass over this bridge before they can be reincarnated.

revision.text:
{{short description|Mythological river in Japanese Buddhist tradition}} [[File:Mitsunobu Sanzu River.jpg|thumb|230px|A depiction of the Sanzu River in [[Tosa Mitsunobu]]'s ''Jūō-zu'' (十王図). The good can cross the river by a bridge while the evil are cast into the dragon-infested rapids.]] The {{nihongo|'''''Sanzu-no-Kawa'''''|||lit. "River of Three Crossings", or the "Sanzu River"}} is a mythological river in [[Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhist]] tradition similar to the [[Chinese mythology|Chinese]] concept of [[Diyu|Huang Quan (Yellow Springs)]], [[Indian religions|Indian]] concept of the [[Vaitarani (mythology)|Vaitarani]] and [[Greek mythology|Greek]] concept of the [[Styx]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=Death and the afterlife in Japanese Buddhism|url=https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston|url-access=limited|date=2008|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|author1=Stone, Jacqueline Ilyse|author1-link=Jacqueline Stone|author2=Walter, Mariko Namba|isbn=978-1-4416-1977-8|location=Honolulu|pages=[https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston/page/n194 184], 259|oclc=657757860}}</ref>
Before reaching the afterlife, the souls of the deceased must cross the river by one of three crossing points: a bridge, a ford, or a stretch of deep, snake-infested waters.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nichirenlibrary.org/en/dic/Content/R/53|title=River of Three Crossings {{!}} Dictionary of Buddhism {{!}} Nichiren Buddhism Library|last=|first=|date=|website=www.nichirenlibrary.org|archive-url=|archive-date=|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref> The weight of one's offenses while alive determines which path an individual must take. It is believed that a toll of six [[Japanese mon (currency)|mon]] must be paid before a soul can cross the river, a belief reflected in [[Japanese funeral]]s when the necessary fee is placed in the casket with the dead.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://matthewmeyer.net/blog/2014/02/23/meido-the-japanese-underworld/|title=Meido: The Japanese Underworld {{!}} Matthew Meyer|date=2014-02-23|language=en-US|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref>
The ''Sanzu River'' is popularly believed to be in [[Mount Osore]], a suitably desolate and remote part of [[Aomori Prefecture]] in northern Japan.
Similarly to the ''Sanzu-no-Kawa'', there is also the {{nihongo|''Sai no Kawara''|||lit. "Riverbed of Death"}}, a boundary by which the souls of children who died too early cross over to the realm of the Dead, with the help of ''[[Jizō]]'', a ''Kami''/''Bodhisattva'' who helps the souls of children who died too early to avoid the attentions of the ''Oni'' and of ''Shozuka-no-Baba'' and ''Datsueba''.
==Real Sanzu Rivers in Japan== * in [[Kanra, Gunma]] {{coord|36.258613|138.952444|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Shirakura River) * in [[Chōnan, Chiba]] {{coord|35.422747|140.264917|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Ichinomiya River) * in [[Zaō, Miyagi]] {{coord|38.144116|140.491333|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Nigori River) * in [[Mutsu, Aomori]] {{coord|41.325877|141.096083|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (drains from Usori Lake)
==See also== * [[Yomotsu Hirasaka]] * [[Yomi]] ** [[Yama]] ** [[Yama (Buddhism)]] ** [[Naraka]] ** [[Ne-no-kuni]] ** Meido * {{nihongo|''[[Higan#Uses and legends|Higan]]''|, lit. "Distant Shore"}} - The other side of the ''Sanzu River'', opposite the Living World's side. * [[Bardo]] - Buddhist mythology * [[Gjöll]] – Norse mythology * [[Hitfun]] - Mandaeism * [[Hubur]] – Mesopotamian mythology * [[Styx]] – Greek mythology * [[Vaitarna River (mythological)]] – [[Indian religions]] * [[Naihe Bridge]] - the entrance and exit to the underworld in Taoism and Chinese folk beliefs; the ghosts must pass over this bridge before they can be reincarnated.
==References== {{Reflist}}
{{Jmyth navbox long}} {{Rivers of Japan}} ]] ]] ]]{{Buddhist-myth-stub}} {{Japan-myth-stub}} {{Japan-river-stub}}

"Distant Shore"}} - The other side of the ''Sanzu River'', opposite the Living World's side. * [[Bardo]] - Buddhist mythology * [[Gjöll]] – Norse mythology * [[Hitfun]] - Mandaeism * [[Hubur]] – Mesopotamian mythology * [[Styx]] – Greek mythology * [[Vaitarna River (mythological)]] – [[Indian religions]] * [[Naihe Bridge]] - the entrance and exit to the underworld in Taoism and Chinese folk beliefs; the ghosts must pass over this bridge before they can be reincarnated.

revision.text:
{{short description|Mythological river in Japanese Buddhist tradition}} [[File:Mitsunobu Sanzu River.jpg|thumb|230px|A depiction of the Sanzu River in [[Tosa Mitsunobu]]'s ''Jūō-zu'' (十王図). The good can cross the river by a bridge while the evil are cast into the dragon-infested rapids.]] The {{nihongo|'''''Sanzu-no-Kawa'''''|||lit. "River of Three Crossings", or the "Sanzu River"}} is a mythological river in [[Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhist]] tradition similar to the [[Chinese mythology|Chinese]] concept of [[Diyu|Huang Quan (Yellow Springs)]], [[Indian religions|Indian]] concept of the [[Vaitarani (mythology)|Vaitarani]] and [[Greek mythology|Greek]] concept of the [[Styx]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=Death and the afterlife in Japanese Buddhism|url=https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston|url-access=limited|date=2008|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|author1=Stone, Jacqueline Ilyse|author1-link=Jacqueline Stone|author2=Walter, Mariko Namba|isbn=978-1-4416-1977-8|location=Honolulu|pages=[https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston/page/n194 184], 259|oclc=657757860}}</ref>
Before reaching the afterlife, the souls of the deceased must cross the river by one of three crossing points: a bridge, a ford, or a stretch of deep, snake-infested waters.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nichirenlibrary.org/en/dic/Content/R/53|title=River of Three Crossings {{!}} Dictionary of Buddhism {{!}} Nichiren Buddhism Library|last=|first=|date=|website=www.nichirenlibrary.org|archive-url=|archive-date=|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref> The weight of one's offenses while alive determines which path an individual must take. It is believed that a toll of six [[Japanese mon (currency)|mon]] must be paid before a soul can cross the river, a belief reflected in [[Japanese funeral]]s when the necessary fee is placed in the casket with the dead.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://matthewmeyer.net/blog/2014/02/23/meido-the-japanese-underworld/|title=Meido: The Japanese Underworld {{!}} Matthew Meyer|date=2014-02-23|language=en-US|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref>
The ''Sanzu River'' is popularly believed to be in [[Mount Osore]], a suitably desolate and remote part of [[Aomori Prefecture]] in northern Japan.
Similarly to the ''Sanzu-no-Kawa'', there is also the {{nihongo|''Sai no Kawara''|||lit. "Riverbed of Death"}}, a boundary by which the souls of children who died too early cross over to the realm of the Dead, with the help of ''[[Jizō]]'', a ''Kami''/''Bodhisattva'' who helps the souls of children who died too early to avoid the attentions of the ''Oni'' and of ''Shozuka-no-Baba'' and ''Datsueba''.
==Real Sanzu Rivers in Japan== * in [[Kanra, Gunma]] {{coord|36.258613|138.952444|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Shirakura River) * in [[Chōnan, Chiba]] {{coord|35.422747|140.264917|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Ichinomiya River) * in [[Zaō, Miyagi]] {{coord|38.144116|140.491333|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Nigori River) * in [[Mutsu, Aomori]] {{coord|41.325877|141.096083|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (drains from Usori Lake)
==See also== * [[Yomotsu Hirasaka]] * [[Yomi]] ** [[Yama]] ** [[Yama (Buddhism)]] ** [[Naraka]] ** [[Ne-no-kuni]] ** Meido * {{nihongo|''[[Higan#Uses and legends|Higan]]''|, lit. "Distant Shore"}} - The other side of the ''Sanzu River'', opposite the Living World's side. * [[Bardo]] - Buddhist mythology * [[Gjöll]] – Norse mythology * [[Hitfun]] - Mandaeism * [[Hubur]] – Mesopotamian mythology * [[Styx]] – Greek mythology * [[Vaitarna River (mythological)]] – [[Indian religions]] * [[Naihe Bridge]] - the entrance and exit to the underworld in Taoism and Chinese folk beliefs; the ghosts must pass over this bridge before they can be reincarnated.
==References== {{Reflist}}
{{Jmyth navbox long}} {{Rivers of Japan}} ]] ]] ]]{{Buddhist-myth-stub}} {{Japan-myth-stub}} {{Japan-river-stub}}

[[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }}

The good can cross the river by a bridge while the evil are cast into the dragon-infested rapids.]] The {{nihongo|'''''Sanzu-no-Kawa'''''|||lit. It is believed that a toll of six [[:en:Japanese mon (currency)|mon]] must be paid before a soul can cross the river, a belief reflected in [[:en:Japanese funeral|Japanese funeral]]s when the necessary fee is placed in the casket with the dead.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://matthewmeyer.net/blog/2014/02/23/meido-the-japanese-underworld/|title=Meido: The Japanese Underworld {{!}} Matthew Meyer|date=2014-02-23|language=en-US|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref>

"Riverbed of Death"}}, a boundary by which the souls of children who died too early cross over to the realm of the Dead, with the help of ''[[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Kṣitigarbha]]'', a ''Kami''/''Bodhisattva'' who helps the souls of children who died too early to avoid the attentions of the ''Oni'' and of ''Shozuka-no-Baba'' and ''Datsueba''.

revision.text:
{{short description|Mythological river in Japanese Buddhist tradition}} [[File:Mitsunobu Sanzu River.jpg|thumb|230px|A depiction of the Sanzu River in [[Tosa Mitsunobu]]'s ''Jūō-zu'' (十王図). The good can cross the river by a bridge while the evil are cast into the dragon-infested rapids.]] The {{nihongo|'''''Sanzu-no-Kawa'''''|||lit. "River of Three Crossings", or the "Sanzu River"}} is a mythological river in [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhist]] tradition similar to the [[:en:Chinese mythology|Chinese]] concept of [[:en:Diyu|Huang Quan (Yellow Springs)]], [[:en:Indian religions|Indian]] concept of the [[Vaitarani (mythology)|Vaitarani]] and [[:en:Greek mythology|Greek]] concept of the [[Styx]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=Death and the afterlife in Japanese Buddhism|url=https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston|url-access=limited|date=2008|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|author1=Stone, Jacqueline Ilyse|author1-link=Jacqueline Stone|author2=Walter, Mariko Namba|isbn=978-1-4416-1977-8|location=Honolulu|pages=[https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston/page/n194 184], 259|oclc=657757860}}</ref>
Before reaching the afterlife, the souls of the deceased must cross the river by one of three crossing points: a bridge, a ford, or a stretch of deep, snake-infested waters.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nichirenlibrary.org/en/dic/Content/R/53|title=River of Three Crossings {{!}} Dictionary of Buddhism {{!}} Nichiren Buddhism Library|last=|first=|date=|website=www.nichirenlibrary.org|archive-url=|archive-date=|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref> The weight of one's offenses while alive determines which path an individual must take. It is believed that a toll of six [[:en:Japanese mon (currency)|mon]] must be paid before a soul can cross the river, a belief reflected in [[:en:Japanese funeral|Japanese funeral]]s when the necessary fee is placed in the casket with the dead.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://matthewmeyer.net/blog/2014/02/23/meido-the-japanese-underworld/|title=Meido: The Japanese Underworld {{!}} Matthew Meyer|date=2014-02-23|language=en-US|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref>
The ''Sanzu River'' is popularly believed to be in [[:en:Mount Osore|Mount Osore]], a suitably desolate and remote part of [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]] in northern Japan.
Similarly to the ''Sanzu-no-Kawa'', there is also the {{nihongo|''Sai no Kawara''|||lit. "Riverbed of Death"}}, a boundary by which the souls of children who died too early cross over to the realm of the Dead, with the help of ''[[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Kṣitigarbha]]'', a ''Kami''/''Bodhisattva'' who helps the souls of children who died too early to avoid the attentions of the ''Oni'' and of ''Shozuka-no-Baba'' and ''Datsueba''.
==Real Sanzu Rivers in Japan== * in [[:en:Kanra, Gunma|Kanra, Gunma]] {{coord|36.258613|138.952444|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Shirakura River) * in [[:en:Chōnan, Chiba|Chōnan, Chiba]] {{coord|35.422747|140.264917|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Ichinomiya River) * in [[Zaō, Miyagi]] {{coord|38.144116|140.491333|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Nigori River) * in [[Mutsu, Aomori]] {{coord|41.325877|141.096083|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (drains from Usori Lake)
==See also== * [[Yomotsu Hirasaka]] * [[Yomi]] ** [[Yama]] ** [[Yama (Buddhism)]] ** [[Naraka]] ** [[:en:Ne-no-kuni|Ne-no-kuni]] ** Meido * {{nihongo|''[[Higan#Uses and legends|Higan]]''|, lit. "Distant Shore"}} - The other side of the ''Sanzu River'', opposite the Living World's side. * [[:en:Bardo|Bardo]] - Buddhist mythology * [[:en:Gjöll|Gjöll]] – Norse mythology * [[:en:Hitfun|Hitfun]] - Mandaeism * [[:en:Hubur|Hubur]] – Mesopotamian mythology * [[Styx]] – Greek mythology * [[Vaitarna River (mythological)]] – [[:en:Indian religions|Indian religions]] * [[Naihe Bridge]] - the entrance and exit to the underworld in Taoism and Chinese folk beliefs; the ghosts must pass over this bridge before they can be reincarnated.
==References== {{Reflist}}
{{Jmyth navbox long}} {{Rivers of Japan}} ]] ]] ]]{{Buddhist-myth-stub}} {{Japan-myth-stub}} {{Japan-river-stub}}

}}

"Riverbed of Death"}}, a boundary by which the souls of children who died too early cross over to the realm of the Dead, with the help of ''[[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Kṣitigarbha]]'', a ''Kami''/''Bodhisattva'' who helps the souls of children who died too early to avoid the attentions of the ''Oni'' and of ''Shozuka-no-Baba'' and ''Datsueba''.
==Real Sanzu Rivers in Japan== * in [[:en:Kanra, Gunma|Kanra, Gunma]] {{coord|36.258613|138.952444|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Shirakura River) * in [[:en:Chōnan, Chiba|Chōnan, Chiba]] {{coord|35.422747|140.264917|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Ichinomiya River) * in [[:en:Zaō, Miyagi|Zaō, Miyagi]] {{coord|38.144116|140.491333|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Nigori River) * in [[Mutsu, Aomori]] {{coord|41.325877|141.096083|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (drains from Usori Lake)

"Distant Shore"}} - The other side of the ''Sanzu River'', opposite the Living World's side. * [[:en:Bardo|Bardo]] - Buddhist mythology * [[:en:Gjöll|Gjöll]] – Norse mythology * [[:en:Hitfun|Hitfun]] - Mandaeism * [[:en:Hubur|Hubur]] – Mesopotamian mythology * [[Styx]] – Greek mythology * [[Vaitarna River (mythological)]] – [[:en:Indian religions|Indian religions]] * [[Naihe Bridge]] - the entrance and exit to the underworld in Taoism and Chinese folk beliefs; the ghosts must pass over this bridge before they can be reincarnated.

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{short description|Mythological river in Japanese Buddhist tradition}} [[File:Mitsunobu Sanzu River.jpg|thumb|230px|A depiction of the Sanzu River in [[Tosa Mitsunobu]]'s ''Jūō-zu'' (十王図). The good can cross the river by a bridge while the evil are cast into the dragon-infested rapids.]] The {{nihongo|'''''Sanzu-no-Kawa'''''|||lit. "River of Three Crossings", or the "Sanzu River"}} is a mythological river in [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhist]] tradition similar to the [[:en:Chinese mythology|Chinese]] concept of [[:en:Diyu|Huang Quan (Yellow Springs)]], [[:en:Indian religions|Indian]] concept of the [[:en:Vaitarani (mythology)|Vaitarani]] and [[:en:Greek mythology|Greek]] concept of the [[Styx]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=Death and the afterlife in Japanese Buddhism|url=https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston|url-access=limited|date=2008|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|author1=Stone, Jacqueline Ilyse|author1-link=Jacqueline Stone|author2=Walter, Mariko Namba|isbn=978-1-4416-1977-8|location=Honolulu|pages=[https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston/page/n194 184], 259|oclc=657757860}}</ref>
Before reaching the afterlife, the souls of the deceased must cross the river by one of three crossing points: a bridge, a ford, or a stretch of deep, snake-infested waters.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nichirenlibrary.org/en/dic/Content/R/53|title=River of Three Crossings {{!}} Dictionary of Buddhism {{!}} Nichiren Buddhism Library|last=|first=|date=|website=www.nichirenlibrary.org|archive-url=|archive-date=|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref> The weight of one's offenses while alive determines which path an individual must take. It is believed that a toll of six [[:en:Japanese mon (currency)|mon]] must be paid before a soul can cross the river, a belief reflected in [[:en:Japanese funeral|Japanese funeral]]s when the necessary fee is placed in the casket with the dead.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://matthewmeyer.net/blog/2014/02/23/meido-the-japanese-underworld/|title=Meido: The Japanese Underworld {{!}} Matthew Meyer|date=2014-02-23|language=en-US|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref>
The ''Sanzu River'' is popularly believed to be in [[:en:Mount Osore|Mount Osore]], a suitably desolate and remote part of [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]] in northern Japan.
Similarly to the ''Sanzu-no-Kawa'', there is also the {{nihongo|''Sai no Kawara''|||lit. "Riverbed of Death"}}, a boundary by which the souls of children who died too early cross over to the realm of the Dead, with the help of ''[[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Kṣitigarbha]]'', a ''Kami''/''Bodhisattva'' who helps the souls of children who died too early to avoid the attentions of the ''Oni'' and of ''Shozuka-no-Baba'' and ''Datsueba''.
==Real Sanzu Rivers in Japan== * in [[:en:Kanra, Gunma|Kanra, Gunma]] {{coord|36.258613|138.952444|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Shirakura River) * in [[:en:Chōnan, Chiba|Chōnan, Chiba]] {{coord|35.422747|140.264917|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Ichinomiya River) * in [[:en:Zaō, Miyagi|Zaō, Miyagi]] {{coord|38.144116|140.491333|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Nigori River) * in [[Mutsu, Aomori]] {{coord|41.325877|141.096083|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (drains from Usori Lake)
==See also== * [[Yomotsu Hirasaka]] * [[Yomi]] ** [[:en:Yama|Yama]] ** [[:en:Yama (Buddhism)|Yama (Buddhism)]] ** [[Naraka]] ** [[:en:Ne-no-kuni|Ne-no-kuni]] ** Meido * {{nihongo|''[[Higan#Uses and legends|Higan]]''|, lit. "Distant Shore"}} - The other side of the ''Sanzu River'', opposite the Living World's side. * [[:en:Bardo|Bardo]] - Buddhist mythology * [[:en:Gjöll|Gjöll]] – Norse mythology * [[:en:Hitfun|Hitfun]] - Mandaeism * [[:en:Hubur|Hubur]] – Mesopotamian mythology * [[Styx]] – Greek mythology * [[:en:Vaitarna River (mythological)|Vaitarna River (mythological)]] – [[:en:Indian religions|Indian religions]] * [[Naihe Bridge]] - the entrance and exit to the underworld in Taoism and Chinese folk beliefs; the ghosts must pass over this bridge before they can be reincarnated.

"Distant Shore"}} - The other side of the ''Sanzu River'', opposite the Living World's side. * [[:en:Bardo|Bardo]] - Buddhist mythology * [[:en:Gjöll|Gjöll]] – Norse mythology * [[:en:Hitfun|Hitfun]] - Mandaeism * [[:en:Hubur|Hubur]] – Mesopotamian mythology * [[:en:Styx|Styx]] – Greek mythology * [[:en:Vaitarna River (mythological)|Vaitarna River (mythological)]] – [[:en:Indian religions|Indian religions]] * [[Naihe Bridge]] - the entrance and exit to the underworld in Taoism and Chinese folk beliefs; the ghosts must pass over this bridge before they can be reincarnated.

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{short description|Mythological river in Japanese Buddhist tradition}} [[File:Mitsunobu Sanzu River.jpg|thumb|230px|A depiction of the Sanzu River in [[:en:Tosa Mitsunobu|Tosa Mitsunobu]]'s ''Jūō-zu'' (十王図). The good can cross the river by a bridge while the evil are cast into the dragon-infested rapids.]] The {{nihongo|'''''Sanzu-no-Kawa'''''|||lit. "River of Three Crossings", or the "Sanzu River"}} is a mythological river in [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhist]] tradition similar to the [[:en:Chinese mythology|Chinese]] concept of [[:en:Diyu|Huang Quan (Yellow Springs)]], [[:en:Indian religions|Indian]] concept of the [[:en:Vaitarani (mythology)|Vaitarani]] and [[:en:Greek mythology|Greek]] concept of the [[:en:Styx|Styx]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=Death and the afterlife in Japanese Buddhism|url=https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston|url-access=limited|date=2008|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|author1=Stone, Jacqueline Ilyse|author1-link=Jacqueline Stone|author2=Walter, Mariko Namba|isbn=978-1-4416-1977-8|location=Honolulu|pages=[https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston/page/n194 184], 259|oclc=657757860}}</ref>
Before reaching the afterlife, the souls of the deceased must cross the river by one of three crossing points: a bridge, a ford, or a stretch of deep, snake-infested waters.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nichirenlibrary.org/en/dic/Content/R/53|title=River of Three Crossings {{!}} Dictionary of Buddhism {{!}} Nichiren Buddhism Library|last=|first=|date=|website=www.nichirenlibrary.org|archive-url=|archive-date=|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref> The weight of one's offenses while alive determines which path an individual must take. It is believed that a toll of six [[:en:Japanese mon (currency)|mon]] must be paid before a soul can cross the river, a belief reflected in [[:en:Japanese funeral|Japanese funeral]]s when the necessary fee is placed in the casket with the dead.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://matthewmeyer.net/blog/2014/02/23/meido-the-japanese-underworld/|title=Meido: The Japanese Underworld {{!}} Matthew Meyer|date=2014-02-23|language=en-US|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref>
The ''Sanzu River'' is popularly believed to be in [[:en:Mount Osore|Mount Osore]], a suitably desolate and remote part of [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]] in northern Japan.
Similarly to the ''Sanzu-no-Kawa'', there is also the {{nihongo|''Sai no Kawara''|||lit. "Riverbed of Death"}}, a boundary by which the souls of children who died too early cross over to the realm of the Dead, with the help of ''[[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Kṣitigarbha]]'', a ''Kami''/''Bodhisattva'' who helps the souls of children who died too early to avoid the attentions of the ''Oni'' and of ''Shozuka-no-Baba'' and ''Datsueba''.
==Real Sanzu Rivers in Japan== * in [[:en:Kanra, Gunma|Kanra, Gunma]] {{coord|36.258613|138.952444|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Shirakura River) * in [[:en:Chōnan, Chiba|Chōnan, Chiba]] {{coord|35.422747|140.264917|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Ichinomiya River) * in [[:en:Zaō, Miyagi|Zaō, Miyagi]] {{coord|38.144116|140.491333|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Nigori River) * in [[Mutsu, Aomori]] {{coord|41.325877|141.096083|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (drains from Usori Lake)
==See also== * [[:en:Yomotsu Hirasaka|Yomotsu Hirasaka]] * [[:en:Yomi|Yomi]] ** [[:en:Yama|Yama]] ** [[:en:Yama (Buddhism)|Yama (Buddhism)]] ** [[Naraka]] ** [[:en:Ne-no-kuni|Ne-no-kuni]] ** Meido * {{nihongo|''[[Higan#Uses and legends|Higan]]''|, lit. "Distant Shore"}} - The other side of the ''Sanzu River'', opposite the Living World's side. * [[:en:Bardo|Bardo]] - Buddhist mythology * [[:en:Gjöll|Gjöll]] – Norse mythology * [[:en:Hitfun|Hitfun]] - Mandaeism * [[:en:Hubur|Hubur]] – Mesopotamian mythology * [[:en:Styx|Styx]] – Greek mythology * [[:en:Vaitarna River (mythological)|Vaitarna River (mythological)]] – [[:en:Indian religions|Indian religions]] * [[Naihe Bridge]] - the entrance and exit to the underworld in Taoism and Chinese folk beliefs; the ghosts must pass over this bridge before they can be reincarnated.
==References== {{Reflist}}
{{Jmyth navbox long}} {{Rivers of Japan}} ]] ]] ]]{{Buddhist-myth-stub}} {{Japan-myth-stub}} {{Japan-river-stub}}

==Real Sanzu Rivers in Japan== * in [[:en:Kanra, Gunma|Kanra, Gunma]] {{coord|36.258613|138.952444|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Shirakura River) * in [[:en:Chōnan, Chiba|Chōnan, Chiba]] {{coord|35.422747|140.264917|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Ichinomiya River) * in [[:en:Zaō, Miyagi|Zaō, Miyagi]] {{coord|38.144116|140.491333|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Nigori River) * in [[Mutsu, Aomori]] {{coord|41.325877|141.096083|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (drains from Usori Lake)
==See also== * [[:en:Yomotsu Hirasaka|Yomotsu Hirasaka]] * [[:en:Yomi|Yomi]] ** [[:en:Yama|Yama]] ** [[:en:Yama (Buddhism)|Yama (Buddhism)]] ** [[Naraka]] ** [[:en:Ne-no-kuni|Ne-no-kuni]] ** Meido * {{nihongo|''[[Higan#Uses and legends|Higan]]''|, lit. "Distant Shore"}} - The other side of the ''Sanzu River'', opposite the Living World's side. * [[:en:Bardo|Bardo]] - Buddhist mythology * [[:en:Gjöll|Gjöll]] – Norse mythology * [[:en:Hitfun|Hitfun]] - Mandaeism * [[:en:Hubur|Hubur]] – Mesopotamian mythology * [[:en:Styx|Styx]] – Greek mythology * [[:en:Vaitarna River (mythological)|Vaitarna River (mythological)]] – [[:en:Indian religions|Indian religions]] * [[Naihe Bridge]] - the entrance and exit to the underworld in Taoism and Chinese folk beliefs; the ghosts must pass over this bridge before they can be reincarnated.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Riu Sanzu]][[sn:commons:Category:Sanzu River]][[en:Sanzu River]][[es:Río Sanzu]][[fi:Sanzu]][[fr:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[hy:Սանձու գետ]][[id:Sungai Sanzu]][[it:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[ja:三途川]][[ko:삼도천]][[nl:Sanzu]][[pt:Rio Sanzu]][[ru:Сандзу]][[sl:Reka Sanzu]][[sv:Sanzu]][[th:แม่น้ำซันซุ]][[zh:三途川]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q2068000]] Wikidata name: Sanzu River [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:06, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Mythological river in Japanese Buddhist tradition}} [[File:Mitsunobu Sanzu River.jpg|thumb|230px|A depiction of the Sanzu River in [[:en:Tosa Mitsunobu|Tosa Mitsunobu]]'s ''Jūō-zu'' (十王図). The good can cross the river by a bridge while the evil are cast into the dragon-infested rapids.]] The {{nihongo|'''''Sanzu-no-Kawa'''''|||lit. "River of Three Crossings", or the "Sanzu River"}} is a mythological river in [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhist]] tradition similar to the [[:en:Chinese mythology|Chinese]] concept of [[:en:Diyu|Huang Quan (Yellow Springs)]], [[:en:Indian religions|Indian]] concept of the [[:en:Vaitarani (mythology)|Vaitarani]] and [[:en:Greek mythology|Greek]] concept of the [[:en:Styx|Styx]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=Death and the afterlife in Japanese Buddhism|url=https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston|url-access=limited|date=2008|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|author1=Stone, Jacqueline Ilyse|author1-link=Jacqueline Stone|author2=Walter, Mariko Namba|isbn=978-1-4416-1977-8|location=Honolulu|pages=[https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston/page/n194 184], 259|oclc=657757860}}</ref>
Before reaching the afterlife, the souls of the deceased must cross the river by one of three crossing points: a bridge, a ford, or a stretch of deep, snake-infested waters.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nichirenlibrary.org/en/dic/Content/R/53|title=River of Three Crossings {{!}} Dictionary of Buddhism {{!}} Nichiren Buddhism Library|last=|first=|date=|website=www.nichirenlibrary.org|archive-url=|archive-date=|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref> The weight of one's offenses while alive determines which path an individual must take. It is believed that a toll of six [[:en:Japanese mon (currency)|mon]] must be paid before a soul can cross the river, a belief reflected in [[:en:Japanese funeral|Japanese funeral]]s when the necessary fee is placed in the casket with the dead.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://matthewmeyer.net/blog/2014/02/23/meido-the-japanese-underworld/|title=Meido: The Japanese Underworld {{!}} Matthew Meyer|date=2014-02-23|language=en-US|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref>
The ''Sanzu River'' is popularly believed to be in [[:en:Mount Osore|Mount Osore]], a suitably desolate and remote part of [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]] in northern Japan.
Similarly to the ''Sanzu-no-Kawa'', there is also the {{nihongo|''Sai no Kawara''|||lit. "Riverbed of Death"}}, a boundary by which the souls of children who died too early cross over to the realm of the Dead, with the help of ''[[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Kṣitigarbha]]'', a ''Kami''/''Bodhisattva'' who helps the souls of children who died too early to avoid the attentions of the ''Oni'' and of ''Shozuka-no-Baba'' and ''Datsueba''.
==Real Sanzu Rivers in Japan== * in [[:en:Kanra, Gunma|Kanra, Gunma]] {{coord|36.258613|138.952444|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Shirakura River) * in [[:en:Chōnan, Chiba|Chōnan, Chiba]] {{coord|35.422747|140.264917|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Ichinomiya River) * in [[:en:Zaō, Miyagi|Zaō, Miyagi]] {{coord|38.144116|140.491333|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Nigori River) * in [[Mutsu, Aomori]] {{coord|41.325877|141.096083|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (drains from Usori Lake)
==See also== * [[:en:Yomotsu Hirasaka|Yomotsu Hirasaka]] * [[:en:Yomi|Yomi]] ** [[:en:Yama|Yama]] ** [[:en:Yama (Buddhism)|Yama (Buddhism)]] ** [[Naraka]] ** [[:en:Ne-no-kuni|Ne-no-kuni]] ** Meido * {{nihongo|''[[Higan#Uses and legends|Higan]]''|, lit. "Distant Shore"}} - The other side of the ''Sanzu River'', opposite the Living World's side. * [[:en:Bardo|Bardo]] - Buddhist mythology * [[:en:Gjöll|Gjöll]] – Norse mythology * [[:en:Hitfun|Hitfun]] - Mandaeism * [[:en:Hubur|Hubur]] – Mesopotamian mythology * [[:en:Styx|Styx]] – Greek mythology * [[:en:Vaitarna River (mythological)|Vaitarna River (mythological)]] – [[:en:Indian religions|Indian religions]] * [[Naihe Bridge]] - the entrance and exit to the underworld in Taoism and Chinese folk beliefs; the ghosts must pass over this bridge before they can be reincarnated.
==References== {{Reflist}}

The good can cross the river by a bridge while the evil are cast into the dragon-infested rapids.]] The {{nihongo|'''''Sanzu-no-Kawa'''''|||lit. "River of Three Crossings", or the "Sanzu River"}} is a mythological river in [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhist]] tradition similar to the [[:en:Chinese mythology|Chinese]] concept of [[:en:Diyu|Huang Quan (Yellow Springs)]], [[:en:Indian religions|Indian]] concept of the [[:en:Vaitarani (mythology)|Vaitarani]] and [[:en:Greek mythology|Greek]] concept of the [[:en:Styx|Styx]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=Death and the afterlife in Japanese Buddhism|url=https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston|url-access=limited|date=2008|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|author1=Stone, Jacqueline Ilyse|author1-link=Jacqueline Stone|author2=Walter, Mariko Namba|isbn=978-1-4416-1977-8|location=Honolulu|pages=[https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston/page/n194 184], 259|oclc=657757860}}</ref>
Before reaching the afterlife, the souls of the deceased must cross the river by one of three crossing points: a bridge, a ford, or a stretch of deep, snake-infested waters.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nichirenlibrary.org/en/dic/Content/R/53|title=River of Three Crossings {{!}} Dictionary of Buddhism {{!}} Nichiren Buddhism Library|last=|first=|date=|website=www.nichirenlibrary.org|archive-url=|archive-date=|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref> The weight of one's offenses while alive determines which path an individual must take. It is believed that a toll of six [[:en:Japanese mon (currency)|mon]] must be paid before a soul can cross the river, a belief reflected in [[:en:Japanese funeral|Japanese funeral]]s when the necessary fee is placed in the casket with the dead.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://matthewmeyer.net/blog/2014/02/23/meido-the-japanese-underworld/|title=Meido: The Japanese Underworld {{!}} Matthew Meyer|date=2014-02-23|language=en-US|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref>
The ''Sanzu River'' is popularly believed to be in [[:en:Mount Osore|Mount Osore]], a suitably desolate and remote part of [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]] in northern Japan.
Similarly to the ''Sanzu-no-Kawa'', there is also the {{nihongo|''Sai no Kawara''|||lit. "Riverbed of Death"}}, a boundary by which the souls of children who died too early cross over to the realm of the Dead, with the help of ''[[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Kṣitigarbha]]'', a ''Kami''/''Bodhisattva'' who helps the souls of children who died too early to avoid the attentions of the ''Oni'' and of ''Shozuka-no-Baba'' and ''Datsueba''.
==Real Sanzu Rivers in Japan== * in [[:en:Kanra, Gunma|Kanra, Gunma]] {{coord|36.258613|138.952444|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Shirakura River) * in [[:en:Chōnan, Chiba|Chōnan, Chiba]] {{coord|35.422747|140.264917|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Ichinomiya River) * in [[:en:Zaō, Miyagi|Zaō, Miyagi]] {{coord|38.144116|140.491333|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Nigori River) * in [[Mutsu, Aomori]] {{coord|41.325877|141.096083|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (drains from Usori Lake)
==See also== * [[:en:Yomotsu Hirasaka|Yomotsu Hirasaka]] * [[:en:Yomi|Yomi]] ** [[:en:Yama|Yama]] ** [[:en:Yama (Buddhism)|Yama (Buddhism)]] ** [[Naraka]] ** [[:en:Ne-no-kuni|Ne-no-kuni]] ** Meido * {{nihongo|''[[Higan#Uses and legends|Higan]]''|, lit. "Distant Shore"}} - The other side of the ''Sanzu River'', opposite the Living World's side. * [[:en:Bardo|Bardo]] - Buddhist mythology * [[:en:Gjöll|Gjöll]] – Norse mythology * [[:en:Hitfun|Hitfun]] - Mandaeism * [[:en:Hubur|Hubur]] – Mesopotamian mythology * [[:en:Styx|Styx]] – Greek mythology * [[:en:Vaitarna River (mythological)|Vaitarna River (mythological)]] – [[:en:Indian religions|Indian religions]] * [[Naihe Bridge]] - the entrance and exit to the underworld in Taoism and Chinese folk beliefs; the ghosts must pass over this bridge before they can be reincarnated.
==References== {{Reflist}}

"Distant Shore"}} - The other side of the ''Sanzu River'', opposite the Living World's side. * [[:en:Bardo|Bardo]] - Buddhist mythology * [[:en:Gjöll|Gjöll]] – Norse mythology * [[:en:Hitfun|Hitfun]] - Mandaeism * [[:en:Hubur|Hubur]] – Mesopotamian mythology * [[:en:Styx|Styx]] – Greek mythology * [[:en:Vaitarna River (mythological)|Vaitarna River (mythological)]] – [[:en:Indian religions|Indian religions]] * [[Naihe Bridge]] - the entrance and exit to the underworld in Taoism and Chinese folk beliefs; the ghosts must pass over this bridge before they can be reincarnated.

[[en:Sanzu River]]
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]]
<!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ca:Riu Sanzu]] {{Commons category|Sanzu River}} [[es:Río Sanzu]] [[fi:Sanzu]] [[fr:Sanzu-no-kawa]] [[hy:Սանձու գետ]] [[id:Sungai Sanzu]] [[it:Sanzu-no-kawa]] [[ja:三途川]] [[ko:삼도천]] [[nl:Sanzu]] [[pt:Rio Sanzu]] [[ru:Сандзу]] [[sl:Reka Sanzu]] [[sv:Sanzu]] [[th:แม่น้ำซันซุ]] [[zh:三途川]] {{wikidata link|Q2068000}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Riu Sanzu]][[sn:commons:Category:Sanzu River]][[en:Sanzu River]][[es:Río Sanzu]][[fi:Sanzu]][[fr:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[hy:Սանձու գետ]][[id:Sungai Sanzu]][[it:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[ja:三途川]][[ko:삼도천]][[nl:Sanzu]][[pt:Rio Sanzu]][[ru:Сандзу]][[sl:Reka Sanzu]][[sv:Sanzu]][[th:แม่น้ำซันซุ]][[zh:三途川]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2068000]] Wikidata name: Sanzu River [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:06, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Mythological river in Japanese Buddhist tradition}} [[File:Mitsunobu Sanzu River.jpg|thumb|230px|A depiction of the Sanzu River in [[:en:Tosa Mitsunobu|Tosa Mitsunobu]]'s ''Jūō-zu'' (十王図). The good can cross the river by a bridge while the evil are cast into the dragon-infested rapids.]] The {{nihongo|'''''Sanzu-no-Kawa'''''|||lit. "River of Three Crossings", or the "Sanzu River"}} is a mythological river in [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhist]] tradition similar to the [[:en:Chinese mythology|Chinese]] concept of [[:en:Diyu|Huang Quan (Yellow Springs)]], [[:en:Indian religions|Indian]] concept of the [[:en:Vaitarani (mythology)|Vaitarani]] and [[:en:Greek mythology|Greek]] concept of the [[:en:Styx|Styx]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=Death and the afterlife in Japanese Buddhism|url=https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston|url-access=limited|date=2008|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|author1=Stone, Jacqueline Ilyse|author1-link=Jacqueline Stone|author2=Walter, Mariko Namba|isbn=978-1-4416-1977-8|location=Honolulu|pages=[https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston/page/n194 184], 259|oclc=657757860}}</ref>
Before reaching the afterlife, the souls of the deceased must cross the river by one of three crossing points: a bridge, a ford, or a stretch of deep, snake-infested waters.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nichirenlibrary.org/en/dic/Content/R/53|title=River of Three Crossings {{!}} Dictionary of Buddhism {{!}} Nichiren Buddhism Library|last=|first=|date=|website=www.nichirenlibrary.org|archive-url=|archive-date=|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref> The weight of one's offenses while alive determines which path an individual must take. It is believed that a toll of six [[:en:Japanese mon (currency)|mon]] must be paid before a soul can cross the river, a belief reflected in [[:en:Japanese funeral|Japanese funeral]]s when the necessary fee is placed in the casket with the dead.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://matthewmeyer.net/blog/2014/02/23/meido-the-japanese-underworld/|title=Meido: The Japanese Underworld {{!}} Matthew Meyer|date=2014-02-23|language=en-US|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref>
The ''Sanzu River'' is popularly believed to be in [[:en:Mount Osore|Mount Osore]], a suitably desolate and remote part of [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]] in northern Japan.
Similarly to the ''Sanzu-no-Kawa'', there is also the {{nihongo|''Sai no Kawara''|||lit. "Riverbed of Death"}}, a boundary by which the souls of children who died too early cross over to the realm of the Dead, with the help of ''[[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Kṣitigarbha]]'', a ''Kami''/''Bodhisattva'' who helps the souls of children who died too early to avoid the attentions of the ''Oni'' and of ''Shozuka-no-Baba'' and ''Datsueba''.
==Real Sanzu Rivers in Japan== * in [[:en:Kanra, Gunma|Kanra, Gunma]] {{coord|36.258613|138.952444|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Shirakura River) * in [[:en:Chōnan, Chiba|Chōnan, Chiba]] {{coord|35.422747|140.264917|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Ichinomiya River) * in [[:en:Zaō, Miyagi|Zaō, Miyagi]] {{coord|38.144116|140.491333|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Nigori River) * in [[Mutsu, Aomori]] {{coord|41.325877|141.096083|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (drains from Usori Lake)
==See also== * [[:en:Yomotsu Hirasaka|Yomotsu Hirasaka]] * [[:en:Yomi|Yomi]] ** [[:en:Yama|Yama]] ** [[:en:Yama (Buddhism)|Yama (Buddhism)]] ** [[Naraka]] ** [[:en:Ne-no-kuni|Ne-no-kuni]] ** Meido * {{nihongo|''[[Higan#Uses and legends|Higan]]''|, lit. "Distant Shore"}} - The other side of the ''Sanzu River'', opposite the Living World's side. * [[:en:Bardo|Bardo]] - Buddhist mythology * [[:en:Gjöll|Gjöll]] – Norse mythology * [[:en:Hitfun|Hitfun]] - Mandaeism * [[:en:Hubur|Hubur]] – Mesopotamian mythology * [[:en:Styx|Styx]] – Greek mythology * [[:en:Vaitarna River (mythological)|Vaitarna River (mythological)]] – [[:en:Indian religions|Indian religions]] * [[Naihe Bridge]] - the entrance and exit to the underworld in Taoism and Chinese folk beliefs; the ghosts must pass over this bridge before they can be reincarnated.
==References== {{Reflist}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Riu Sanzu]][[commons:Category:Sanzu River]][[en:Sanzu River]][[es:Río Sanzu]][[fi:Sanzu]][[fr:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[hy:Սանձու գետ]][[id:Sungai Sanzu]][[it:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[ja:三途川]][[ko:삼도천]][[nl:Sanzu]][[pt:Rio Sanzu]][[ru:Сандзу]][[sl:Reka Sanzu]][[sv:Sanzu]][[th:แม่น้ำซันซุ]][[zh:三途川]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Riu Sanzu]][[commons:Category:Sanzu River]][[en:Sanzu River]][[es:Río Sanzu]][[fi:Sanzu]][[fr:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[hy:Սանձու գետ]][[id:Sungai Sanzu]][[it:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[ja:三途川]][[ko:삼도천]][[nl:Sanzu]][[pt:Rio Sanzu]][[ru:Сандзу]][[sl:Reka Sanzu]][[sv:Sanzu]][[th:แม่น้ำซันซุ]][[zh:三途川]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Riu Sanzu]][[sn:commons:Category:Sanzu River]][[en:Sanzu River]][[es:Río Sanzu]][[fi:Sanzu]][[fr:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[hy:Սանձու գետ]][[id:Sungai Sanzu]][[it:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[ja:三途川]][[ko:삼도천]][[nl:Sanzu]][[pt:Rio Sanzu]][[ru:Сандзу]][[sl:Reka Sanzu]][[sv:Sanzu]][[th:แม่น้ำซันซุ]][[zh:三途川]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2068000]] Wikidata name: Sanzu River [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:06, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Mythological river in Japanese Buddhist tradition}} [[File:Mitsunobu Sanzu River.jpg|thumb|230px|A depiction of the Sanzu River in [[:en:Tosa Mitsunobu|Tosa Mitsunobu]]'s ''Jūō-zu'' (十王図). The good can cross the river by a bridge while the evil are cast into the dragon-infested rapids.]] The {{nihongo|'''''Sanzu-no-Kawa'''''|||lit. "River of Three Crossings", or the "Sanzu River"}} is a mythological river in [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhist]] tradition similar to the [[:en:Chinese mythology|Chinese]] concept of [[:en:Diyu|Huang Quan (Yellow Springs)]], [[:en:Indian religions|Indian]] concept of the [[:en:Vaitarani (mythology)|Vaitarani]] and [[:en:Greek mythology|Greek]] concept of the [[:en:Styx|Styx]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=Death and the afterlife in Japanese Buddhism|url=https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston|url-access=limited|date=2008|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|author1=Stone, Jacqueline Ilyse|author1-link=Jacqueline Stone|author2=Walter, Mariko Namba|isbn=978-1-4416-1977-8|location=Honolulu|pages=[https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston/page/n194 184], 259|oclc=657757860}}</ref>
Before reaching the afterlife, the souls of the deceased must cross the river by one of three crossing points: a bridge, a ford, or a stretch of deep, snake-infested waters.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nichirenlibrary.org/en/dic/Content/R/53|title=River of Three Crossings {{!}} Dictionary of Buddhism {{!}} Nichiren Buddhism Library|last=|first=|date=|website=www.nichirenlibrary.org|archive-url=|archive-date=|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref> The weight of one's offenses while alive determines which path an individual must take. It is believed that a toll of six [[:en:Japanese mon (currency)|mon]] must be paid before a soul can cross the river, a belief reflected in [[:en:Japanese funeral|Japanese funeral]]s when the necessary fee is placed in the casket with the dead.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://matthewmeyer.net/blog/2014/02/23/meido-the-japanese-underworld/|title=Meido: The Japanese Underworld {{!}} Matthew Meyer|date=2014-02-23|language=en-US|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref>
The ''Sanzu River'' is popularly believed to be in [[:en:Mount Osore|Mount Osore]], a suitably desolate and remote part of [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]] in northern Japan.
Similarly to the ''Sanzu-no-Kawa'', there is also the {{nihongo|''Sai no Kawara''|||lit. "Riverbed of Death"}}, a boundary by which the souls of children who died too early cross over to the realm of the Dead, with the help of ''[[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Kṣitigarbha]]'', a ''Kami''/''Bodhisattva'' who helps the souls of children who died too early to avoid the attentions of the ''Oni'' and of ''Shozuka-no-Baba'' and ''Datsueba''.
==Real Sanzu Rivers in Japan== * in [[:en:Kanra, Gunma|Kanra, Gunma]] {{coord|36.258613|138.952444|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Shirakura River) * in [[:en:Chōnan, Chiba|Chōnan, Chiba]] {{coord|35.422747|140.264917|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Ichinomiya River) * in [[:en:Zaō, Miyagi|Zaō, Miyagi]] {{coord|38.144116|140.491333|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Nigori River) * in [[Mutsu, Aomori]] {{coord|41.325877|141.096083|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (drains from Usori Lake)
==See also== * [[:en:Yomotsu Hirasaka|Yomotsu Hirasaka]] * [[:en:Yomi|Yomi]] ** [[:en:Yama|Yama]] ** [[:en:Yama (Buddhism)|Yama (Buddhism)]] ** [[Naraka]] ** [[:en:Ne-no-kuni|Ne-no-kuni]] ** Meido * {{nihongo|''[[Higan#Uses and legends|Higan]]''|, lit. "Distant Shore"}} - The other side of the ''Sanzu River'', opposite the Living World's side. * [[:en:Bardo|Bardo]] - Buddhist mythology * [[:en:Gjöll|Gjöll]] – Norse mythology * [[:en:Hitfun|Hitfun]] - Mandaeism * [[:en:Hubur|Hubur]] – Mesopotamian mythology * [[:en:Styx|Styx]] – Greek mythology * [[:en:Vaitarna River (mythological)|Vaitarna River (mythological)]] – [[:en:Indian religions|Indian religions]] * [[Naihe Bridge]] - the entrance and exit to the underworld in Taoism and Chinese folk beliefs; the ghosts must pass over this bridge before they can be reincarnated.
==References== {{Reflist}}
{{Jmyth navbox long}} {{Rivers of Japan}}]] ]]{{Buddhist-myth-stub}} {{Japan-myth-stub}} {{Japan-river-stub}}
