<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Riu Sanzu]][[commons:Category:Sanzu River]][[en:Sanzu River]][[es:Río Sanzu]][[fi:Sanzu]][[fr:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[hy:Սանձու գետ]][[id:Sungai Sanzu]][[it:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[ja:三途川]][[ko:삼도천]][[nl:Sanzu]][[pt:Rio Sanzu]][[ru:Сандзу]][[sl:Reka Sanzu]][[sv:Sanzu]][[th:แม่น้ำซันซุ]][[zh:三途川]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Riu Sanzu]][[commons:Category:Sanzu River]][[en:Sanzu River]][[es:Río Sanzu]][[fi:Sanzu]][[fr:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[hy:Սանձու գետ]][[id:Sungai Sanzu]][[it:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[ja:三途川]][[ko:삼도천]][[nl:Sanzu]][[pt:Rio Sanzu]][[ru:Сандзу]][[sl:Reka Sanzu]][[sv:Sanzu]][[th:แม่น้ำซันซุ]][[zh:三途川]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Riu Sanzu]][[sn:commons:Category:Sanzu River]][[en:Sanzu River]][[es:Río Sanzu]][[fi:Sanzu]][[fr:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[hy:Սանձու գետ]][[id:Sungai Sanzu]][[it:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[ja:三途川]][[ko:삼도천]][[nl:Sanzu]][[pt:Rio Sanzu]][[ru:Сандзу]][[sl:Reka Sanzu]][[sv:Sanzu]][[th:แม่น้ำซันซุ]][[zh:三途川]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2068000]] Wikidata name: Sanzu River [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:06, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Mythological river in Japanese Buddhist tradition}} [[File:Mitsunobu Sanzu River.jpg|thumb|230px|A depiction of the Sanzu River in [[:en:Tosa Mitsunobu|Tosa Mitsunobu]]'s ''Jūō-zu'' (十王図). The good can cross the river by a bridge while the evil are cast into the dragon-infested rapids.]] The {{nihongo|'''''Sanzu-no-Kawa'''''|||lit. "River of Three Crossings", or the "Sanzu River"}} is a mythological river in [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhist]] tradition similar to the [[:en:Chinese mythology|Chinese]] concept of [[:en:Diyu|Huang Quan (Yellow Springs)]], [[:en:Indian religions|Indian]] concept of the [[:en:Vaitarani (mythology)|Vaitarani]] and [[:en:Greek mythology|Greek]] concept of the [[:en:Styx|Styx]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=Death and the afterlife in Japanese Buddhism|url=https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston|url-access=limited|date=2008|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|author1=Stone, Jacqueline Ilyse|author1-link=Jacqueline Stone|author2=Walter, Mariko Namba|isbn=978-1-4416-1977-8|location=Honolulu|pages=[https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston/page/n194 184], 259|oclc=657757860}}</ref>
Before reaching the afterlife, the souls of the deceased must cross the river by one of three crossing points: a bridge, a ford, or a stretch of deep, snake-infested waters.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nichirenlibrary.org/en/dic/Content/R/53|title=River of Three Crossings {{!}} Dictionary of Buddhism {{!}} Nichiren Buddhism Library|last=|first=|date=|website=www.nichirenlibrary.org|archive-url=|archive-date=|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref> The weight of one's offenses while alive determines which path an individual must take. It is believed that a toll of six [[:en:Japanese mon (currency)|mon]] must be paid before a soul can cross the river, a belief reflected in [[:en:Japanese funeral|Japanese funeral]]s when the necessary fee is placed in the casket with the dead.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://matthewmeyer.net/blog/2014/02/23/meido-the-japanese-underworld/|title=Meido: The Japanese Underworld {{!}} Matthew Meyer|date=2014-02-23|language=en-US|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref>
The ''Sanzu River'' is popularly believed to be in [[:en:Mount Osore|Mount Osore]], a suitably desolate and remote part of [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]] in northern Japan.
Similarly to the ''Sanzu-no-Kawa'', there is also the {{nihongo|''Sai no Kawara''|||lit. "Riverbed of Death"}}, a boundary by which the souls of children who died too early cross over to the realm of the Dead, with the help of ''[[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Kṣitigarbha]]'', a ''Kami''/''Bodhisattva'' who helps the souls of children who died too early to avoid the attentions of the ''Oni'' and of ''Shozuka-no-Baba'' and ''Datsueba''.
==Real Sanzu Rivers in Japan== * in [[:en:Kanra, Gunma|Kanra, Gunma]] {{coord|36.258613|138.952444|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Shirakura River) * in [[:en:Chōnan, Chiba|Chōnan, Chiba]] {{coord|35.422747|140.264917|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Ichinomiya River) * in [[:en:Zaō, Miyagi|Zaō, Miyagi]] {{coord|38.144116|140.491333|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Nigori River) * in [[Mutsu, Aomori]] {{coord|41.325877|141.096083|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (drains from Usori Lake)
==See also== * [[:en:Yomotsu Hirasaka|Yomotsu Hirasaka]] * [[:en:Yomi|Yomi]] ** [[:en:Yama|Yama]] ** [[:en:Yama (Buddhism)|Yama (Buddhism)]] ** [[Naraka]] ** [[:en:Ne-no-kuni|Ne-no-kuni]] ** Meido * {{nihongo|''[[Higan#Uses and legends|Higan]]''|, lit. "Distant Shore"}} - The other side of the ''Sanzu River'', opposite the Living World's side. * [[:en:Bardo|Bardo]] - Buddhist mythology * [[:en:Gjöll|Gjöll]] – Norse mythology * [[:en:Hitfun|Hitfun]] - Mandaeism * [[:en:Hubur|Hubur]] – Mesopotamian mythology * [[:en:Styx|Styx]] – Greek mythology * [[:en:Vaitarna River (mythological)|Vaitarna River (mythological)]] – [[:en:Indian religions|Indian religions]] * [[Naihe Bridge]] - the entrance and exit to the underworld in Taoism and Chinese folk beliefs; the ghosts must pass over this bridge before they can be reincarnated.
==References== {{Reflist}}
{{Jmyth navbox long}} {{Rivers of Japan}}]] ]]{{Buddhist-myth-stub}} {{Japan-myth-stub}} {{Japan-river-stub}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Riu Sanzu]][[co:commons:Category:Sanzu River]][[en:Sanzu River]][[es:Río Sanzu]][[fi:Sanzu]][[fr:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[hy:Սանձու գետ]][[id:Sungai Sanzu]][[it:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[ja:三途川]][[ko:삼도천]][[nl:Sanzu]][[pt:Rio Sanzu]][[ru:Сандзу]][[sl:Reka Sanzu]][[sv:Sanzu]][[th:แม่น้ำซันซุ]][[zh:三途川]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Riu Sanzu]][[co:commons:Category:Sanzu River]][[en:Sanzu River]][[es:Río Sanzu]][[fi:Sanzu]][[fr:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[hy:Սանձու գետ]][[id:Sungai Sanzu]][[it:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[ja:三途川]][[ko:삼도천]][[nl:Sanzu]][[pt:Rio Sanzu]][[ru:Сандзу]][[sl:Reka Sanzu]][[sv:Sanzu]][[th:แม่น้ำซันซุ]][[zh:三途川]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Riu Sanzu]][[sn:commons:Category:Sanzu River]][[en:Sanzu River]][[es:Río Sanzu]][[fi:Sanzu]][[fr:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[hy:Սանձու գետ]][[id:Sungai Sanzu]][[it:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[ja:三途川]][[ko:삼도천]][[nl:Sanzu]][[pt:Rio Sanzu]][[ru:Сандзу]][[sl:Reka Sanzu]][[sv:Sanzu]][[th:แม่น้ำซันซุ]][[zh:三途川]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2068000]] Wikidata name: Sanzu River [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:06, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Mythological river in Japanese Buddhist tradition}} [[File:Mitsunobu Sanzu River.jpg|thumb|230px|A depiction of the Sanzu River in [[:en:Tosa Mitsunobu|Tosa Mitsunobu]]'s ''Jūō-zu'' (十王図). The good can cross the river by a bridge while the evil are cast into the dragon-infested rapids.]] The {{nihongo|'''''Sanzu-no-Kawa'''''|||lit. "River of Three Crossings", or the "Sanzu River"}} is a mythological river in [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhist]] tradition similar to the [[:en:Chinese mythology|Chinese]] concept of [[:en:Diyu|Huang Quan (Yellow Springs)]], [[:en:Indian religions|Indian]] concept of the [[:en:Vaitarani (mythology)|Vaitarani]] and [[:en:Greek mythology|Greek]] concept of the [[:en:Styx|Styx]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=Death and the afterlife in Japanese Buddhism|url=https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston|url-access=limited|date=2008|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|author1=Stone, Jacqueline Ilyse|author1-link=Jacqueline Stone|author2=Walter, Mariko Namba|isbn=978-1-4416-1977-8|location=Honolulu|pages=[https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston/page/n194 184], 259|oclc=657757860}}</ref>
Before reaching the afterlife, the souls of the deceased must cross the river by one of three crossing points: a bridge, a ford, or a stretch of deep, snake-infested waters.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nichirenlibrary.org/en/dic/Content/R/53|title=River of Three Crossings {{!}} Dictionary of Buddhism {{!}} Nichiren Buddhism Library|last=|first=|date=|website=www.nichirenlibrary.org|archive-url=|archive-date=|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref> The weight of one's offenses while alive determines which path an individual must take. It is believed that a toll of six [[:en:Japanese mon (currency)|mon]] must be paid before a soul can cross the river, a belief reflected in [[:en:Japanese funeral|Japanese funeral]]s when the necessary fee is placed in the casket with the dead.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://matthewmeyer.net/blog/2014/02/23/meido-the-japanese-underworld/|title=Meido: The Japanese Underworld {{!}} Matthew Meyer|date=2014-02-23|language=en-US|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref>
The ''Sanzu River'' is popularly believed to be in [[:en:Mount Osore|Mount Osore]], a suitably desolate and remote part of [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]] in northern Japan.
Similarly to the ''Sanzu-no-Kawa'', there is also the {{nihongo|''Sai no Kawara''|||lit. "Riverbed of Death"}}, a boundary by which the souls of children who died too early cross over to the realm of the Dead, with the help of ''[[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Kṣitigarbha]]'', a ''Kami''/''Bodhisattva'' who helps the souls of children who died too early to avoid the attentions of the ''Oni'' and of ''Shozuka-no-Baba'' and ''Datsueba''.
==Real Sanzu Rivers in Japan== * in [[:en:Kanra, Gunma|Kanra, Gunma]] {{coord|36.258613|138.952444|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Shirakura River) * in [[:en:Chōnan, Chiba|Chōnan, Chiba]] {{coord|35.422747|140.264917|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Ichinomiya River) * in [[:en:Zaō, Miyagi|Zaō, Miyagi]] {{coord|38.144116|140.491333|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Nigori River) * in [[Mutsu, Aomori]] {{coord|41.325877|141.096083|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (drains from Usori Lake)
==See also== * [[:en:Yomotsu Hirasaka|Yomotsu Hirasaka]] * [[:en:Yomi|Yomi]] ** [[:en:Yama|Yama]] ** [[:en:Yama (Buddhism)|Yama (Buddhism)]] ** [[Naraka]] ** [[:en:Ne-no-kuni|Ne-no-kuni]] ** Meido * {{nihongo|''[[Higan#Uses and legends|Higan]]''|, lit. "Distant Shore"}} - The other side of the ''Sanzu River'', opposite the Living World's side. * [[:en:Bardo|Bardo]] - Buddhist mythology * [[:en:Gjöll|Gjöll]] – Norse mythology * [[:en:Hitfun|Hitfun]] - Mandaeism * [[:en:Hubur|Hubur]] – Mesopotamian mythology * [[:en:Styx|Styx]] – Greek mythology * [[:en:Vaitarna River (mythological)|Vaitarna River (mythological)]] – [[:en:Indian religions|Indian religions]] * [[Naihe Bridge]] - the entrance and exit to the underworld in Taoism and Chinese folk beliefs; the ghosts must pass over this bridge before they can be reincarnated.
==References== {{Reflist}}
{{Jmyth navbox long}} {{Rivers of Japan}}]] ]]{{Buddhist-myth-stub}} {{Japan-myth-stub}} {{Japan-river-stub}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Riu Sanzu]][[co:commons:Category:Sanzu River]][[en:Sanzu River]][[es:Río Sanzu]][[fi:Sanzu]][[fr:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[hy:Սանձու գետ]][[id:Sungai Sanzu]][[it:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[ja:三途川]][[ko:삼도천]][[nl:Sanzu]][[pt:Rio Sanzu]][[ru:Сандзу]][[sl:Reka Sanzu]][[sv:Sanzu]][[th:แม่น้ำซันซุ]][[zh:三途川]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Riu Sanzu]][[co:commons:Category:Sanzu River]][[en:Sanzu River]][[es:Río Sanzu]][[fi:Sanzu]][[fr:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[hy:Սանձու գետ]][[id:Sungai Sanzu]][[it:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[ja:三途川]][[ko:삼도천]][[nl:Sanzu]][[pt:Rio Sanzu]][[ru:Сандзу]][[sl:Reka Sanzu]][[sv:Sanzu]][[th:แม่น้ำซันซุ]][[zh:三途川]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Riu Sanzu]][[sn:commons:Category:Sanzu River]][[en:Sanzu River]][[es:Río Sanzu]][[fi:Sanzu]][[fr:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[hy:Սանձու գետ]][[id:Sungai Sanzu]][[it:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[ja:三途川]][[ko:삼도천]][[nl:Sanzu]][[pt:Rio Sanzu]][[ru:Сандзу]][[sl:Reka Sanzu]][[sv:Sanzu]][[th:แม่น้ำซันซุ]][[zh:三途川]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2068000]] Wikidata name: Sanzu River [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:06, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Mythological river in Japanese Buddhist tradition}} [[File:Mitsunobu Sanzu River.jpg|thumb|230px|A depiction of the Sanzu River in {{ill|Tosa Mitsunobu|en|Tosa Mitsunobu|WD=Q3042277|lt=Tosa Mitsunobu}}'s ''Jūō-zu'' (十王図). The good can cross the river by a bridge while the evil are cast into the dragon-infested rapids.]] The {{nihongo|'''''Sanzu-no-Kawa'''''|||lit. "River of Three Crossings", or the "Sanzu River"}} is a mythological river in {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Japanese Buddhist}} tradition similar to the {{ill|Chinese mythology|en|Chinese mythology|WD=Q210677|lt=Chinese}} concept of {{ill|Diyu|en|Diyu|WD=Q1899406|lt=Huang Quan (Yellow Springs)}}, {{ill|Indian religions|en|Indian religions|WD=Q1651597|lt=Indian}} concept of the {{ill|Vaitarani (mythology)|en|Vaitarani (mythology)|WD=Q3630026|lt=Vaitarani}} and {{ill|Greek mythology|en|Greek mythology|WD=Q34726|lt=Greek}} concept of the {{ill|Styx|en|Styx|WD=Q542758|lt=Styx}}.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Death and the afterlife in Japanese Buddhism|url=https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston|url-access=limited|date=2008|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|author1=Stone, Jacqueline Ilyse|author1-link=Jacqueline Stone|author2=Walter, Mariko Namba|isbn=978-1-4416-1977-8|location=Honolulu|pages=[https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston/page/n194 184], 259|oclc=657757860}}</ref>
Before reaching the afterlife, the souls of the deceased must cross the river by one of three crossing points: a bridge, a ford, or a stretch of deep, snake-infested waters.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nichirenlibrary.org/en/dic/Content/R/53|title=River of Three Crossings {{!}} Dictionary of Buddhism {{!}} Nichiren Buddhism Library|last=|first=|date=|website=www.nichirenlibrary.org|archive-url=|archive-date=|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref> The weight of one's offenses while alive determines which path an individual must take. It is believed that a toll of six {{ill|Japanese mon (currency)|en|Japanese mon (currency)|WD=Q2751502|lt=mon}} must be paid before a soul can cross the river, a belief reflected in {{ill|Japanese funeral|en|Japanese funeral|WD=Q3433318|lt=Japanese funeral}}s when the necessary fee is placed in the casket with the dead.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://matthewmeyer.net/blog/2014/02/23/meido-the-japanese-underworld/|title=Meido: The Japanese Underworld {{!}} Matthew Meyer|date=2014-02-23|language=en-US|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref>
The ''Sanzu River'' is popularly believed to be in {{ill|Mount Osore|en|Mount Osore|WD=Q1377333|lt=Mount Osore}}, a suitably desolate and remote part of {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture}} in northern Japan.
Similarly to the ''Sanzu-no-Kawa'', there is also the {{nihongo|''Sai no Kawara''|||lit. "Riverbed of Death"}}, a boundary by which the souls of children who died too early cross over to the realm of the Dead, with the help of ''{{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|WD=Q604687|lt=Kṣitigarbha}}'', a ''Kami''/''Bodhisattva'' who helps the souls of children who died too early to avoid the attentions of the ''Oni'' and of ''Shozuka-no-Baba'' and ''Datsueba''.
==Real Sanzu Rivers in Japan== * in {{ill|Kanra, Gunma|en|Kanra, Gunma|WD=Q1203523|lt=Kanra, Gunma}} {{coord|36.258613|138.952444|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Shirakura River) * in [[:en:Chōnan, Chiba|Chōnan, Chiba]] {{coord|35.422747|140.264917|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Ichinomiya River) * in {{ill|Zaō, Miyagi|en|Zaō, Miyagi|WD=Q1347874|lt=Zaō, Miyagi}} {{coord|38.144116|140.491333|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Nigori River) * in [[Mutsu, Aomori]] {{coord|41.325877|141.096083|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (drains from Usori Lake)
==See also== * {{ill|Yomotsu Hirasaka|en|Yomotsu Hirasaka|WD=Q22084535|lt=Yomotsu Hirasaka}} * {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|WD=Q1142558|lt=Yomi}} ** {{ill|Yama|en|Yama|WD=Q45097|lt=Yama}} ** {{ill|Yama (Buddhism)|en|Yama (Buddhism)|WD=Q2089602|lt=Yama (Buddhism)}} ** [[Naraka]] ** {{ill|Ne-no-kuni|en|Ne-no-kuni|WD=Q7555425|lt=Ne-no-kuni}} ** Meido * {{nihongo|''[[Higan#Uses and legends|Higan]]''|, lit. "Distant Shore"}} - The other side of the ''Sanzu River'', opposite the Living World's side. * {{ill|Bardo|en|Bardo|WD=Q256585|lt=Bardo}} - Buddhist mythology * {{ill|Gjöll|en|Gjöll|WD=Q1500292|lt=Gjöll}} – Norse mythology * {{ill|Hitfun|en|Hitfun|WD=Q109974549|lt=Hitfun}} - Mandaeism * {{ill|Hubur|en|Hubur|WD=Q425426|lt=Hubur}} – Mesopotamian mythology * {{ill|Styx|en|Styx|WD=Q542758|lt=Styx}} – Greek mythology * [[:en:Vaitarna River (mythological)|Vaitarna River (mythological)]] – {{ill|Indian religions|en|Indian religions|WD=Q1651597|lt=Indian religions}} * [[Naihe Bridge]] - the entrance and exit to the underworld in Taoism and Chinese folk beliefs; the ghosts must pass over this bridge before they can be reincarnated.
==References== {{Reflist}}
{{Jmyth navbox long}} {{Rivers of Japan}}]] ]]{{Buddhist-myth-stub}} {{Japan-myth-stub}} {{Japan-river-stub}}

Similarly to the ''Sanzu-no-Kawa'', there is also the {{nihongo|''Sai no Kawara''|||lit. "Riverbed of Death"}}, a boundary by which the souls of children who died too early cross over to the realm of the Dead, with the help of ''{{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|WD=Q604687|lt=Kṣitigarbha}}'', a ''Kami''/''Bodhisattva'' who helps the souls of children who died too early to avoid the attentions of the ''Oni'' and of ''Shozuka-no-Baba'' and ''Datsueba''.
==Real Sanzu Rivers in Japan== * in {{ill|Kanra, Gunma|en|Kanra, Gunma|WD=Q1203523|lt=Kanra, Gunma}} {{coord|36.258613|138.952444|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Shirakura River) * in [[:en:Chōnan, Chiba|Chōnan, Chiba]] {{coord|35.422747|140.264917|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Ichinomiya River) * in {{ill|Zaō, Miyagi|en|Zaō, Miyagi|WD=Q1347874|lt=Zaō, Miyagi}} {{coord|38.144116|140.491333|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Nigori River) * in [[Mutsu, Aomori]] {{coord|41.325877|141.096083|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (drains from Usori Lake)
==See also== * {{ill|Yomotsu Hirasaka|en|Yomotsu Hirasaka|WD=Q22084535|lt=Yomotsu Hirasaka}} * {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|WD=Q1142558|lt=Yomi}} ** {{ill|Yama|en|Yama|WD=Q45097|lt=Yama}} ** {{ill|Yama (Buddhism)|en|Yama (Buddhism)|WD=Q2089602|lt=Yama (Buddhism)}} ** [[Naraka]] ** {{ill|Ne-no-kuni|en|Ne-no-kuni|WD=Q7555425|lt=Ne-no-kuni}} ** Meido * {{nihongo|''[[Higan#Uses and legends|Higan]]''|, lit. "Distant Shore"}} - The other side of the ''Sanzu River'', opposite the Living World's side. * {{ill|Bardo|en|Bardo|WD=Q256585|lt=Bardo}} - Buddhist mythology * {{ill|Gjöll|en|Gjöll|WD=Q1500292|lt=Gjöll}} – Norse mythology * {{ill|Hitfun|en|Hitfun|WD=Q109974549|lt=Hitfun}} - Mandaeism * {{ill|Hubur|en|Hubur|WD=Q425426|lt=Hubur}} – Mesopotamian mythology * {{ill|Styx|en|Styx|WD=Q542758|lt=Styx}} – Greek mythology * [[:en:Vaitarna River (mythological)|Vaitarna River (mythological)]] – {{ill|Indian religions|en|Indian religions|WD=Q1651597|lt=Indian religions}} * [[Naihe Bridge]] - the entrance and exit to the underworld in Taoism and Chinese folk beliefs; the ghosts must pass over this bridge before they can be reincarnated.
==References== {{Reflist}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Riu Sanzu]][[co:commons:Category:Sanzu River]][[en:Sanzu River]][[es:Río Sanzu]][[fi:Sanzu]][[fr:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[hy:Սանձու գետ]][[id:Sungai Sanzu]][[it:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[ja:三途川]][[ko:삼도천]][[nl:Sanzu]][[pt:Rio Sanzu]][[ru:Сандзу]][[sl:Reka Sanzu]][[sv:Sanzu]][[th:แม่น้ำซันซุ]][[zh:三途川]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Riu Sanzu]][[co:commons:Category:Sanzu River]][[en:Sanzu River]][[es:Río Sanzu]][[fi:Sanzu]][[fr:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[hy:Սանձու գետ]][[id:Sungai Sanzu]][[it:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[ja:三途川]][[ko:삼도천]][[nl:Sanzu]][[pt:Rio Sanzu]][[ru:Сандзу]][[sl:Reka Sanzu]][[sv:Sanzu]][[th:แม่น้ำซันซุ]][[zh:三途川]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Riu Sanzu]][[sn:commons:Category:Sanzu River]][[en:Sanzu River]][[es:Río Sanzu]][[fi:Sanzu]][[fr:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[hy:Սանձու գետ]][[id:Sungai Sanzu]][[it:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[ja:三途川]][[ko:삼도천]][[nl:Sanzu]][[pt:Rio Sanzu]][[ru:Сандзу]][[sl:Reka Sanzu]][[sv:Sanzu]][[th:แม่น้ำซันซุ]][[zh:三途川]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2068000]] Wikidata name: Sanzu River [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:06, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Mythological river in Japanese Buddhist tradition}} [[File:Mitsunobu Sanzu River.jpg|thumb|230px|A depiction of the Sanzu River in {{ill|Tosa Mitsunobu|en|Tosa Mitsunobu|WD=Q3042277|lt=Tosa Mitsunobu}}'s ''Jūō-zu'' (十王図). The good can cross the river by a bridge while the evil are cast into the dragon-infested rapids.]] The {{nihongo|'''''Sanzu-no-Kawa'''''|||lit. "River of Three Crossings", or the "Sanzu River"}} is a mythological river in {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Japanese Buddhist}} tradition similar to the {{ill|Chinese mythology|en|Chinese mythology|WD=Q210677|lt=Chinese}} concept of {{ill|Diyu|en|Diyu|WD=Q1899406|lt=Huang Quan (Yellow Springs)}}, {{ill|Indian religions|en|Indian religions|WD=Q1651597|lt=Indian}} concept of the {{ill|Vaitarani (mythology)|en|Vaitarani (mythology)|WD=Q3630026|lt=Vaitarani}} and {{ill|Greek mythology|en|Greek mythology|WD=Q34726|lt=Greek}} concept of the {{ill|Styx|en|Styx|WD=Q542758|lt=Styx}}.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Death and the afterlife in Japanese Buddhism|url=https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston|url-access=limited|date=2008|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|author1=Stone, Jacqueline Ilyse|author1-link=Jacqueline Stone|author2=Walter, Mariko Namba|isbn=978-1-4416-1977-8|location=Honolulu|pages=[https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston/page/n194 184], 259|oclc=657757860}}</ref>
Before reaching the afterlife, the souls of the deceased must cross the river by one of three crossing points: a bridge, a ford, or a stretch of deep, snake-infested waters.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nichirenlibrary.org/en/dic/Content/R/53|title=River of Three Crossings {{!}} Dictionary of Buddhism {{!}} Nichiren Buddhism Library|last=|first=|date=|website=www.nichirenlibrary.org|archive-url=|archive-date=|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref> The weight of one's offenses while alive determines which path an individual must take. It is believed that a toll of six {{ill|Japanese mon (currency)|en|Japanese mon (currency)|WD=Q2751502|lt=mon}} must be paid before a soul can cross the river, a belief reflected in {{ill|Japanese funeral|en|Japanese funeral|WD=Q3433318|lt=Japanese funeral}}s when the necessary fee is placed in the casket with the dead.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://matthewmeyer.net/blog/2014/02/23/meido-the-japanese-underworld/|title=Meido: The Japanese Underworld {{!}} Matthew Meyer|date=2014-02-23|language=en-US|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref>
The ''Sanzu River'' is popularly believed to be in {{ill|Mount Osore|en|Mount Osore|WD=Q1377333|lt=Mount Osore}}, a suitably desolate and remote part of {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture}} in northern Japan.
Similarly to the ''Sanzu-no-Kawa'', there is also the {{nihongo|''Sai no Kawara''|||lit. "Riverbed of Death"}}, a boundary by which the souls of children who died too early cross over to the realm of the Dead, with the help of ''{{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|WD=Q604687|lt=Kṣitigarbha}}'', a ''Kami''/''Bodhisattva'' who helps the souls of children who died too early to avoid the attentions of the ''Oni'' and of ''Shozuka-no-Baba'' and ''Datsueba''.
==Real Sanzu Rivers in Japan== * in {{ill|Kanra, Gunma|en|Kanra, Gunma|WD=Q1203523|lt=Kanra, Gunma}} {{coord|36.258613|138.952444|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Shirakura River) * in [[:en:Chōnan, Chiba|Chōnan, Chiba]] {{coord|35.422747|140.264917|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Ichinomiya River) * in {{ill|Zaō, Miyagi|en|Zaō, Miyagi|WD=Q1347874|lt=Zaō, Miyagi}} {{coord|38.144116|140.491333|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Nigori River) * in [[Mutsu, Aomori]] {{coord|41.325877|141.096083|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (drains from Usori Lake)
==See also== * {{ill|Yomotsu Hirasaka|en|Yomotsu Hirasaka|WD=Q22084535|lt=Yomotsu Hirasaka}} * {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|WD=Q1142558|lt=Yomi}} ** {{ill|Yama|en|Yama|WD=Q45097|lt=Yama}} ** {{ill|Yama (Buddhism)|en|Yama (Buddhism)|WD=Q2089602|lt=Yama (Buddhism)}} ** [[Naraka]] ** {{ill|Ne-no-kuni|en|Ne-no-kuni|WD=Q7555425|lt=Ne-no-kuni}} ** Meido * {{nihongo|''[[Higan#Uses and legends|Higan]]''|, lit. "Distant Shore"}} - The other side of the ''Sanzu River'', opposite the Living World's side. * {{ill|Bardo|en|Bardo|WD=Q256585|lt=Bardo}} - Buddhist mythology * {{ill|Gjöll|en|Gjöll|WD=Q1500292|lt=Gjöll}} – Norse mythology * {{ill|Hitfun|en|Hitfun|WD=Q109974549|lt=Hitfun}} - Mandaeism * {{ill|Hubur|en|Hubur|WD=Q425426|lt=Hubur}} – Mesopotamian mythology * {{ill|Styx|en|Styx|WD=Q542758|lt=Styx}} – Greek mythology * [[:en:Vaitarna River (mythological)|Vaitarna River (mythological)]] – {{ill|Indian religions|en|Indian religions|WD=Q1651597|lt=Indian religions}} * [[Naihe Bridge]] - the entrance and exit to the underworld in Taoism and Chinese folk beliefs; the ghosts must pass over this bridge before they can be reincarnated.

{{short description|Mythological river in Japanese Buddhist tradition}} [[File:Mitsunobu Sanzu River.jpg|thumb|230px|A depiction of the Sanzu River in {{ill|Tosa Mitsunobu|en|Tosa Mitsunobu|WD=Q3042277|lt=Tosa Mitsunobu}}'s ''Jūō-zu'' (十王図). The good can cross the river by a bridge while the evil are cast into the dragon-infested rapids.]] The {{nihongo|'''''Sanzu-no-Kawa'''''|||lit. "River of Three Crossings", or the "Sanzu River"}} is a mythological river in {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Japanese Buddhist}} tradition similar to the {{ill|Chinese mythology|en|Chinese mythology|WD=Q210677|lt=Chinese}} concept of {{ill|Diyu|en|Diyu|WD=Q1899406|lt=Huang Quan (Yellow Springs)}}, {{ill|Indian religions|en|Indian religions|WD=Q1651597|lt=Indian}} concept of the {{ill|Vaitarani (mythology)|en|Vaitarani (mythology)|WD=Q3630026|lt=Vaitarani}} and {{ill|Greek mythology|en|Greek mythology|WD=Q34726|lt=Greek}} concept of the {{ill|Styx|en|Styx|WD=Q542758|lt=Styx}}.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Death and the afterlife in Japanese Buddhism|url=https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston|url-access=limited|date=2008|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|author1=Stone, Jacqueline Ilyse|author1-link=Jacqueline Stone|author2=Walter, Mariko Namba|isbn=978-1-4416-1977-8|location=Honolulu|pages=[https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston/page/n194 184], 259|oclc=657757860}}</ref>
Before reaching the afterlife, the souls of the deceased must cross the river by one of three crossing points: a bridge, a ford, or a stretch of deep, snake-infested waters.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nichirenlibrary.org/en/dic/Content/R/53|title=River of Three Crossings {{!}} Dictionary of Buddhism {{!}} Nichiren Buddhism Library|last=|first=|date=|website=www.nichirenlibrary.org|archive-url=|archive-date=|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref> The weight of one's offenses while alive determines which path an individual must take. It is believed that a toll of six {{ill|Japanese mon (currency)|en|Japanese mon (currency)|WD=Q2751502|lt=mon}} must be paid before a soul can cross the river, a belief reflected in {{ill|Japanese funeral|en|Japanese funeral|WD=Q3433318|lt=Japanese funeral}}s when the necessary fee is placed in the casket with the dead.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://matthewmeyer.net/blog/2014/02/23/meido-the-japanese-underworld/|title=Meido: The Japanese Underworld {{!}} Matthew Meyer|date=2014-02-23|language=en-US|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref>
The ''Sanzu River'' is popularly believed to be in {{ill|Mount Osore|en|Mount Osore|WD=Q1377333|lt=Mount Osore}}, a suitably desolate and remote part of {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture}} in northern Japan.
Similarly to the ''Sanzu-no-Kawa'', there is also the {{nihongo|''Sai no Kawara''|||lit. "Riverbed of Death"}}, a boundary by which the souls of children who died too early cross over to the realm of the Dead, with the help of ''{{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|WD=Q604687|lt=Kṣitigarbha}}'', a ''Kami''/''Bodhisattva'' who helps the souls of children who died too early to avoid the attentions of the ''Oni'' and of ''Shozuka-no-Baba'' and ''Datsueba''.
==Real Sanzu Rivers in Japan== * in {{ill|Kanra, Gunma|en|Kanra, Gunma|WD=Q1203523|lt=Kanra, Gunma}} {{coord|36.258613|138.952444|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Shirakura River) * in [[:en:Chōnan, Chiba|Chōnan, Chiba]] {{coord|35.422747|140.264917|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Ichinomiya River) * in {{ill|Zaō, Miyagi|en|Zaō, Miyagi|WD=Q1347874|lt=Zaō, Miyagi}} {{coord|38.144116|140.491333|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Nigori River) * in [[Mutsu, Aomori]] {{coord|41.325877|141.096083|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (drains from Usori Lake)
==See also== * {{ill|Yomotsu Hirasaka|en|Yomotsu Hirasaka|WD=Q22084535|lt=Yomotsu Hirasaka}} * {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|WD=Q1142558|lt=Yomi}} ** {{ill|Yama|en|Yama|WD=Q45097|lt=Yama}} ** {{ill|Yama (Buddhism)|en|Yama (Buddhism)|WD=Q2089602|lt=Yama (Buddhism)}} ** [[Naraka]] ** {{ill|Ne-no-kuni|en|Ne-no-kuni|WD=Q7555425|lt=Ne-no-kuni}} ** Meido * {{nihongo|''[[Higan#Uses and legends|Higan]]''|, lit. "Distant Shore"}} - The other side of the ''Sanzu River'', opposite the Living World's side. * {{ill|Bardo|en|Bardo|WD=Q256585|lt=Bardo}} - Buddhist mythology * {{ill|Gjöll|en|Gjöll|WD=Q1500292|lt=Gjöll}} – Norse mythology * {{ill|Hitfun|en|Hitfun|WD=Q109974549|lt=Hitfun}} - Mandaeism * {{ill|Hubur|en|Hubur|WD=Q425426|lt=Hubur}} – Mesopotamian mythology * {{ill|Styx|en|Styx|WD=Q542758|lt=Styx}} – Greek mythology * [[:en:Vaitarna River (mythological)|Vaitarna River (mythological)]] – {{ill|Indian religions|en|Indian religions|WD=Q1651597|lt=Indian religions}} * [[Naihe Bridge]] - the entrance and exit to the underworld in Taoism and Chinese folk beliefs; the ghosts must pass over this bridge before they can be reincarnated.
==References== {{Reflist}}

"Riverbed of Death"}}, a boundary by which the souls of children who died too early cross over to the realm of the Dead, with the help of ''{{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|WD=Q604687|lt=Kṣitigarbha|1=Kṣitigarbha}}'', a ''Kami''/''Bodhisattva'' who helps the souls of children who died too early to avoid the attentions of the ''Oni'' and of ''Shozuka-no-Baba'' and ''Datsueba''.

==See also== * {{ill|Yomotsu Hirasaka|en|Yomotsu Hirasaka|WD=Q22084535|lt=Yomotsu Hirasaka|1=Yomotsu Hirasaka}} * {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|WD=Q1142558|lt=Yomi|1=Yomi}} ** {{ill|Yama|en|Yama|WD=Q45097|lt=Yama|1=Yama}} ** {{ill|Yama (Buddhism)|en|Yama (Buddhism)|WD=Q2089602|lt=Yama (Buddhism)|1=Yama (Buddhism)}} ** [[Naraka]] ** {{ill|Ne-no-kuni|en|Ne-no-kuni|WD=Q7555425|lt=Ne-no-kuni|1=Ne-no-kuni}} ** Meido * {{nihongo|''[[Higan#Uses and legends|Higan]]''|, lit. "Distant Shore"}} - The other side of the ''Sanzu River'', opposite the Living World's side. * {{ill|Bardo|en|Bardo|WD=Q256585|lt=Bardo|1=Bardo}} - Buddhist mythology * {{ill|Gjöll|en|Gjöll|WD=Q1500292|lt=Gjöll|1=Gjöll}} – Norse mythology * {{ill|Hitfun|en|Hitfun|WD=Q109974549|lt=Hitfun|1=Hitfun}} - Mandaeism * {{ill|Hubur|en|Hubur|WD=Q425426|lt=Hubur|1=Hubur}} – Mesopotamian mythology * {{ill|Styx|en|Styx|WD=Q542758|lt=Styx|1=Styx}} – Greek mythology * [[:en:Vaitarna River (mythological)|Vaitarna River (mythological)]] – {{ill|Indian religions|en|Indian religions|WD=Q1651597|lt=Indian religions|1=Indian religions}} * [[Naihe Bridge]] - the entrance and exit to the underworld in Taoism and Chinese folk beliefs; the ghosts must pass over this bridge before they can be reincarnated.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Riu Sanzu]][[co:commons:Category:Sanzu River]][[en:Sanzu River]][[es:Río Sanzu]][[fi:Sanzu]][[fr:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[hy:Սանձու գետ]][[id:Sungai Sanzu]][[it:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[ja:三途川]][[ko:삼도천]][[nl:Sanzu]][[pt:Rio Sanzu]][[ru:Сандзу]][[sl:Reka Sanzu]][[sv:Sanzu]][[th:แม่น้ำซันซุ]][[zh:三途川]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Riu Sanzu]][[co:commons:Category:Sanzu River]][[en:Sanzu River]][[es:Río Sanzu]][[fi:Sanzu]][[fr:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[hy:Սանձու գետ]][[id:Sungai Sanzu]][[it:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[ja:三途川]][[ko:삼도천]][[nl:Sanzu]][[pt:Rio Sanzu]][[ru:Сандзу]][[sl:Reka Sanzu]][[sv:Sanzu]][[th:แม่น้ำซันซุ]][[zh:三途川]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Riu Sanzu]][[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Sanzu River]][[en:Sanzu River]][[es:Río Sanzu]][[fi:Sanzu]][[fr:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[hy:Սանձու գետ]][[id:Sungai Sanzu]][[it:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[ja:三途川]][[ko:삼도천]][[nl:Sanzu]][[pt:Rio Sanzu]][[ru:Сандзу]][[sl:Reka Sanzu]][[sv:Sanzu]][[th:แม่น้ำซันซุ]][[zh:三途川]]
{{short description|Mythological river in Japanese Buddhist tradition}} [[File:Mitsunobu Sanzu River.jpg|thumb|230px|A depiction of the Sanzu River in {{ill|Tosa Mitsunobu|en|Tosa Mitsunobu|WD=Q3042277|lt=Tosa Mitsunobu|1=Tosa Mitsunobu}}'s ''Jūō-zu'' (十王図). The good can cross the river by a bridge while the evil are cast into the dragon-infested rapids.]] The {{nihongo|'''''Sanzu-no-Kawa'''''|||lit. "River of Three Crossings", or the "Sanzu River"}} is a mythological river in {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Japanese Buddhist|1=Buddhism in Japan}} tradition similar to the {{ill|Chinese mythology|en|Chinese mythology|WD=Q210677|lt=Chinese|1=Chinese mythology}} concept of {{ill|Diyu|en|Diyu|WD=Q1899406|lt=Huang Quan (Yellow Springs)|1=Diyu}}, {{ill|Indian religions|en|Indian religions|WD=Q1651597|lt=Indian|1=Indian religions}} concept of the {{ill|Vaitarani (mythology)|en|Vaitarani (mythology)|WD=Q3630026|lt=Vaitarani|1=Vaitarani (mythology)}} and {{ill|Greek mythology|en|Greek mythology|WD=Q34726|lt=Greek|1=Greek mythology}} concept of the {{ill|Styx|en|Styx|WD=Q542758|lt=Styx|1=Styx}}.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Death and the afterlife in Japanese Buddhism|url=https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston|url-access=limited|date=2008|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|author1=Stone, Jacqueline Ilyse|author1-link=Jacqueline Stone|author2=Walter, Mariko Namba|isbn=978-1-4416-1977-8|location=Honolulu|pages=[https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston/page/n194 184], 259|oclc=657757860}}</ref>
Before reaching the afterlife, the souls of the deceased must cross the river by one of three crossing points: a bridge, a ford, or a stretch of deep, snake-infested waters.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nichirenlibrary.org/en/dic/Content/R/53|title=River of Three Crossings {{!}} Dictionary of Buddhism {{!}} Nichiren Buddhism Library|last=|first=|date=|website=www.nichirenlibrary.org|archive-url=|archive-date=|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref> The weight of one's offenses while alive determines which path an individual must take. It is believed that a toll of six {{ill|Japanese mon (currency)|en|Japanese mon (currency)|WD=Q2751502|lt=mon|1=Japanese mon (currency)}} must be paid before a soul can cross the river, a belief reflected in {{ill|Japanese funeral|en|Japanese funeral|WD=Q3433318|lt=Japanese funeral|1=Japanese funeral}}s when the necessary fee is placed in the casket with the dead.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://matthewmeyer.net/blog/2014/02/23/meido-the-japanese-underworld/|title=Meido: The Japanese Underworld {{!}} Matthew Meyer|date=2014-02-23|language=en-US|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref>
The ''Sanzu River'' is popularly believed to be in {{ill|Mount Osore|en|Mount Osore|WD=Q1377333|lt=Mount Osore|1=Mount Osore}}, a suitably desolate and remote part of {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture|1=Aomori Prefecture}} in northern Japan.
Similarly to the ''Sanzu-no-Kawa'', there is also the {{nihongo|''Sai no Kawara''|||lit. "Riverbed of Death"}}, a boundary by which the souls of children who died too early cross over to the realm of the Dead, with the help of ''{{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|WD=Q604687|lt=Kṣitigarbha|1=Kṣitigarbha}}'', a ''Kami''/''Bodhisattva'' who helps the souls of children who died too early to avoid the attentions of the ''Oni'' and of ''Shozuka-no-Baba'' and ''Datsueba''.
==Real Sanzu Rivers in Japan== * in {{ill|Kanra, Gunma|en|Kanra, Gunma|WD=Q1203523|lt=Kanra, Gunma|1=Kanra, Gunma}} {{coord|36.258613|138.952444|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Shirakura River) * in [[:en:Chōnan, Chiba|Chōnan, Chiba]] {{coord|35.422747|140.264917|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Ichinomiya River) * in {{ill|Zaō, Miyagi|en|Zaō, Miyagi|WD=Q1347874|lt=Zaō, Miyagi|1=Zaō, Miyagi}} {{coord|38.144116|140.491333|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Nigori River) * in [[Mutsu, Aomori]] {{coord|41.325877|141.096083|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (drains from Usori Lake)
==See also== * {{ill|Yomotsu Hirasaka|en|Yomotsu Hirasaka|WD=Q22084535|lt=Yomotsu Hirasaka|1=Yomotsu Hirasaka}} * {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|WD=Q1142558|lt=Yomi|1=Yomi}} ** {{ill|Yama|en|Yama|WD=Q45097|lt=Yama|1=Yama}} ** {{ill|Yama (Buddhism)|en|Yama (Buddhism)|WD=Q2089602|lt=Yama (Buddhism)|1=Yama (Buddhism)}} ** [[Naraka]] ** {{ill|Ne-no-kuni|en|Ne-no-kuni|WD=Q7555425|lt=Ne-no-kuni|1=Ne-no-kuni}} ** Meido * {{nihongo|''[[Higan#Uses and legends|Higan]]''|, lit. "Distant Shore"}} - The other side of the ''Sanzu River'', opposite the Living World's side. * {{ill|Bardo|en|Bardo|WD=Q256585|lt=Bardo|1=Bardo}} - Buddhist mythology * {{ill|Gjöll|en|Gjöll|WD=Q1500292|lt=Gjöll|1=Gjöll}} – Norse mythology * {{ill|Hitfun|en|Hitfun|WD=Q109974549|lt=Hitfun|1=Hitfun}} - Mandaeism * {{ill|Hubur|en|Hubur|WD=Q425426|lt=Hubur|1=Hubur}} – Mesopotamian mythology * {{ill|Styx|en|Styx|WD=Q542758|lt=Styx|1=Styx}} – Greek mythology * [[:en:Vaitarna River (mythological)|Vaitarna River (mythological)]] – {{ill|Indian religions|en|Indian religions|WD=Q1651597|lt=Indian religions|1=Indian religions}} * [[Naihe Bridge]] - the entrance and exit to the underworld in Taoism and Chinese folk beliefs; the ghosts must pass over this bridge before they can be reincarnated.
==References== {{Reflist}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Riu Sanzu]][[co:commons:Category:Sanzu River]][[en:Sanzu River]][[es:Río Sanzu]][[fi:Sanzu]][[fr:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[hy:Սանձու գետ]][[id:Sungai Sanzu]][[it:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[ja:三途川]][[ko:삼도천]][[nl:Sanzu]][[pt:Rio Sanzu]][[ru:Сандзу]][[sl:Reka Sanzu]][[sv:Sanzu]][[th:แม่น้ำซันซุ]][[zh:三途川]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Riu Sanzu]][[co:commons:Category:Sanzu River]][[en:Sanzu River]][[es:Río Sanzu]][[fi:Sanzu]][[fr:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[hy:Սանձու գետ]][[id:Sungai Sanzu]][[it:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[ja:三途川]][[ko:삼도천]][[nl:Sanzu]][[pt:Rio Sanzu]][[ru:Сандзу]][[sl:Reka Sanzu]][[sv:Sanzu]][[th:แม่น้ำซันซุ]][[zh:三途川]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Riu Sanzu]][[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Sanzu River]][[en:Sanzu River]][[es:Río Sanzu]][[fi:Sanzu]][[fr:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[hy:Սանձու գետ]][[id:Sungai Sanzu]][[it:Sanzu-no-kawa]][[ja:三途川]][[ko:삼도천]][[nl:Sanzu]][[pt:Rio Sanzu]][[ru:Сандзу]][[sl:Reka Sanzu]][[sv:Sanzu]][[th:แม่น้ำซันซุ]][[zh:三途川]]
{{short description|Mythological river in Japanese Buddhist tradition}} [[File:Mitsunobu Sanzu River.jpg|thumb|230px|A depiction of the Sanzu River in {{ill|Tosa Mitsunobu|en|Tosa Mitsunobu|WD=Q3042277|lt=Tosa Mitsunobu|1=Tosa Mitsunobu}}'s ''Jūō-zu'' (十王図). The good can cross the river by a bridge while the evil are cast into the dragon-infested rapids.]] The {{nihongo|'''''Sanzu-no-Kawa'''''|||lit. "River of Three Crossings", or the "Sanzu River"}} is a mythological river in {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Japanese Buddhist|1=Buddhism in Japan}} tradition similar to the {{ill|Chinese mythology|en|Chinese mythology|WD=Q210677|lt=Chinese|1=Chinese mythology}} concept of {{ill|Diyu|en|Diyu|WD=Q1899406|lt=Huang Quan (Yellow Springs)|1=Diyu}}, {{ill|Indian religions|en|Indian religions|WD=Q1651597|lt=Indian|1=Indian religions}} concept of the {{ill|Vaitarani (mythology)|en|Vaitarani (mythology)|WD=Q3630026|lt=Vaitarani|1=Vaitarani (mythology)}} and {{ill|Greek mythology|en|Greek mythology|WD=Q34726|lt=Greek|1=Greek mythology}} concept of the {{ill|Styx|en|Styx|WD=Q542758|lt=Styx|1=Styx}}.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Death and the afterlife in Japanese Buddhism|url=https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston|url-access=limited|date=2008|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|author1=Stone, Jacqueline Ilyse|author1-link=Jacqueline Stone|author2=Walter, Mariko Namba|isbn=978-1-4416-1977-8|location=Honolulu|pages=[https://archive.org/details/deathafterlifeja00ston/page/n194 184], 259|oclc=657757860}}</ref>
Before reaching the afterlife, the souls of the deceased must cross the river by one of three crossing points: a bridge, a ford, or a stretch of deep, snake-infested waters.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nichirenlibrary.org/en/dic/Content/R/53|title=River of Three Crossings {{!}} Dictionary of Buddhism {{!}} Nichiren Buddhism Library|last=|first=|date=|website=www.nichirenlibrary.org|archive-url=|archive-date=|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref> The weight of one's offenses while alive determines which path an individual must take. It is believed that a toll of six {{ill|Japanese mon (currency)|en|Japanese mon (currency)|WD=Q2751502|lt=mon|1=Japanese mon (currency)}} must be paid before a soul can cross the river, a belief reflected in {{ill|Japanese funeral|en|Japanese funeral|WD=Q3433318|lt=Japanese funeral|1=Japanese funeral}}s when the necessary fee is placed in the casket with the dead.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://matthewmeyer.net/blog/2014/02/23/meido-the-japanese-underworld/|title=Meido: The Japanese Underworld {{!}} Matthew Meyer|date=2014-02-23|language=en-US|access-date=2019-12-07}}</ref>
The ''Sanzu River'' is popularly believed to be in {{ill|Mount Osore|en|Mount Osore|WD=Q1377333|lt=Mount Osore|1=Mount Osore}}, a suitably desolate and remote part of {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture|1=Aomori Prefecture}} in northern Japan.
Similarly to the ''Sanzu-no-Kawa'', there is also the {{nihongo|''Sai no Kawara''|||lit. "Riverbed of Death"}}, a boundary by which the souls of children who died too early cross over to the realm of the Dead, with the help of ''{{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|WD=Q604687|lt=Kṣitigarbha|1=Kṣitigarbha}}'', a ''Kami''/''Bodhisattva'' who helps the souls of children who died too early to avoid the attentions of the ''Oni'' and of ''Shozuka-no-Baba'' and ''Datsueba''.
==Real Sanzu Rivers in Japan== * in {{ill|Kanra, Gunma|en|Kanra, Gunma|WD=Q1203523|lt=Kanra, Gunma|1=Kanra, Gunma}} {{coord|36.258613|138.952444|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Shirakura River) * in [[:en:Chōnan, Chiba|Chōnan, Chiba]] {{coord|35.422747|140.264917|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Ichinomiya River) * in {{ill|Zaō, Miyagi|en|Zaō, Miyagi|WD=Q1347874|lt=Zaō, Miyagi|1=Zaō, Miyagi}} {{coord|38.144116|140.491333|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (confluence with Nigori River) * in [[Mutsu, Aomori]] {{coord|41.325877|141.096083|format=dms|display=inline|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} (drains from Usori Lake)
==See also== * {{ill|Yomotsu Hirasaka|en|Yomotsu Hirasaka|WD=Q22084535|lt=Yomotsu Hirasaka|1=Yomotsu Hirasaka}} * {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|WD=Q1142558|lt=Yomi|1=Yomi}} ** {{ill|Yama|en|Yama|WD=Q45097|lt=Yama|1=Yama}} ** {{ill|Yama (Buddhism)|en|Yama (Buddhism)|WD=Q2089602|lt=Yama (Buddhism)|1=Yama (Buddhism)}} ** [[Naraka]] ** {{ill|Ne-no-kuni|en|Ne-no-kuni|WD=Q7555425|lt=Ne-no-kuni|1=Ne-no-kuni}} ** Meido * {{nihongo|''[[Higan#Uses and legends|Higan]]''|, lit. "Distant Shore"}} - The other side of the ''Sanzu River'', opposite the Living World's side. * {{ill|Bardo|en|Bardo|WD=Q256585|lt=Bardo|1=Bardo}} - Buddhist mythology * {{ill|Gjöll|en|Gjöll|WD=Q1500292|lt=Gjöll|1=Gjöll}} – Norse mythology * {{ill|Hitfun|en|Hitfun|WD=Q109974549|lt=Hitfun|1=Hitfun}} - Mandaeism * {{ill|Hubur|en|Hubur|WD=Q425426|lt=Hubur|1=Hubur}} – Mesopotamian mythology * {{ill|Styx|en|Styx|WD=Q542758|lt=Styx|1=Styx}} – Greek mythology * [[:en:Vaitarna River (mythological)|Vaitarna River (mythological)]] – {{ill|Indian religions|en|Indian religions|WD=Q1651597|lt=Indian religions|1=Indian religions}} * [[Naihe Bridge]] - the entrance and exit to the underworld in Taoism and Chinese folk beliefs; the ghosts must pass over this bridge before they can be reincarnated.
==References== {{Reflist}}
